6699						(flags & CHECKPOINT_CAUSE_TIME) ? " time" : "",
6700						(flags & CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL) ? " flush-all" : "")));
6701	else
6702		ereport(LOG,
6703		/* translator: the placeholders show checkpoint options */
6704				(errmsg("checkpoint starting:%s%s%s%s%s%s%s%s",
6705						(flags & CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN) ? " shutdown" : "",
6706						(flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY) ? " end-of-recovery" : "",
6707						(flags & CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE) ? " immediate" : "",
6708						(flags & CHECKPOINT_FORCE) ? " force" : "",
6709						(flags & CHECKPOINT_WAIT) ? " wait" : "",
6710						(flags & CHECKPOINT_CAUSE_XLOG) ? " wal" : "",
6711						(flags & CHECKPOINT_CAUSE_TIME) ? " time" : "",
6712						(flags & CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL) ? " flush-all" : "")));
6713}
6714
6715/*
6716 * Log end of a checkpoint.
6717 */
6718static void
6719LogCheckpointEnd(bool restartpoint)
6720{
6721	long		write_msecs,
6722				sync_msecs,
6723				total_msecs,
6724				longest_msecs,
6725				average_msecs;
6726	uint64		average_sync_time;
6727
6728	CheckpointStats.ckpt_end_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
6729
6730	write_msecs = TimestampDifferenceMilliseconds(CheckpointStats.ckpt_write_t,
6731												  CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_t);
6732
6733	sync_msecs = TimestampDifferenceMilliseconds(CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_t,
6734												 CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_end_t);
6735
6736	/* Accumulate checkpoint timing summary data, in milliseconds. */
6737	PendingCheckpointerStats.write_time += write_msecs;
6738	PendingCheckpointerStats.sync_time += sync_msecs;
6739
6740	/*
6741	 * All of the published timing statistics are accounted for.  Only
6742	 * continue if a log message is to be written.
6743	 */
6744	if (!log_checkpoints)
6745		return;
6746
6747	total_msecs = TimestampDifferenceMilliseconds(CheckpointStats.ckpt_start_t,
6748												  CheckpointStats.ckpt_end_t);
6749
6750	/*
6751	 * Timing values returned from CheckpointStats are in microseconds.
6752	 * Convert to milliseconds for consistent printing.
6753	 */
6754	longest_msecs = (long) ((CheckpointStats.ckpt_longest_sync + 999) / 1000);
6755
6756	average_sync_time = 0;
6757	if (CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_rels > 0)
6758		average_sync_time = CheckpointStats.ckpt_agg_sync_time /
6759			CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_rels;
6760	average_msecs = (long) ((average_sync_time + 999) / 1000);
6761
6762	/*
6763	 * ControlFileLock is not required to see ControlFile->checkPoint and
6764	 * ->checkPointCopy here as we are the only updator of those variables at
6765	 * this moment.
6766	 */
6767	if (restartpoint)
6768		ereport(LOG,
6769				(errmsg("restartpoint complete: wrote %d buffers (%.1f%%), "
6770						"wrote %d SLRU buffers; %d WAL file(s) added, "
6771						"%d removed, %d recycled; write=%ld.%03d s, "
6772						"sync=%ld.%03d s, total=%ld.%03d s; sync files=%d, "
6773						"longest=%ld.%03d s, average=%ld.%03d s; distance=%d kB, "
6774						"estimate=%d kB; lsn=%X/%X, redo lsn=%X/%X",
6775						CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written,
6776						(double) CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written * 100 / NBuffers,
6777						CheckpointStats.ckpt_slru_written,
6778						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_added,
6779						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_removed,
6780						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_recycled,
6781						write_msecs / 1000, (int) (write_msecs % 1000),
6782						sync_msecs / 1000, (int) (sync_msecs % 1000),
6783						total_msecs / 1000, (int) (total_msecs % 1000),
6784						CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_rels,
6785						longest_msecs / 1000, (int) (longest_msecs % 1000),
6786						average_msecs / 1000, (int) (average_msecs % 1000),
6787						(int) (PrevCheckPointDistance / 1024.0),
6788						(int) (CheckPointDistanceEstimate / 1024.0),
6789						LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(ControlFile->checkPoint),
6790						LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo))));
6791	else
6792		ereport(LOG,
6793				(errmsg("checkpoint complete: wrote %d buffers (%.1f%%), "
6794						"wrote %d SLRU buffers; %d WAL file(s) added, "
6795						"%d removed, %d recycled; write=%ld.%03d s, "
6796						"sync=%ld.%03d s, total=%ld.%03d s; sync files=%d, "
6797						"longest=%ld.%03d s, average=%ld.%03d s; distance=%d kB, "
6798						"estimate=%d kB; lsn=%X/%X, redo lsn=%X/%X",
6799						CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written,
6800						(double) CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written * 100 / NBuffers,
6801						CheckpointStats.ckpt_slru_written,
6802						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_added,
6803						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_removed,
6804						CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_recycled,
6805						write_msecs / 1000, (int) (write_msecs % 1000),
6806						sync_msecs / 1000, (int) (sync_msecs % 1000),
6807						total_msecs / 1000, (int) (total_msecs % 1000),
6808						CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_rels,
6809						longest_msecs / 1000, (int) (longest_msecs % 1000),
6810						average_msecs / 1000, (int) (average_msecs % 1000),
6811						(int) (PrevCheckPointDistance / 1024.0),
6812						(int) (CheckPointDistanceEstimate / 1024.0),
6813						LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(ControlFile->checkPoint),
6814						LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo))));
6815}
6816
6817/*
6818 * Update the estimate of distance between checkpoints.
6819 *
6820 * The estimate is used to calculate the number of WAL segments to keep
6821 * preallocated, see XLOGfileslop().
6822 */
6823static void
6824UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate(uint64 nbytes)
6825{
6826	/*
6827	 * To estimate the number of segments consumed between checkpoints, keep a
6828	 * moving average of the amount of WAL generated in previous checkpoint
6829	 * cycles. However, if the load is bursty, with quiet periods and busy
6830	 * periods, we want to cater for the peak load. So instead of a plain
6831	 * moving average, let the average decline slowly if the previous cycle
6832	 * used less WAL than estimated, but bump it up immediately if it used
6833	 * more.
6834	 *
6835	 * When checkpoints are triggered by max_wal_size, this should converge to
6836	 * CheckpointSegments * wal_segment_size,
6837	 *
6838	 * Note: This doesn't pay any attention to what caused the checkpoint.
6839	 * Checkpoints triggered manually with CHECKPOINT command, or by e.g.
6840	 * starting a base backup, are counted the same as those created
6841	 * automatically. The slow-decline will largely mask them out, if they are
6842	 * not frequent. If they are frequent, it seems reasonable to count them
6843	 * in as any others; if you issue a manual checkpoint every 5 minutes and
6844	 * never let a timed checkpoint happen, it makes sense to base the
6845	 * preallocation on that 5 minute interval rather than whatever
6846	 * checkpoint_timeout is set to.
6847	 */
6848	PrevCheckPointDistance = nbytes;
6849	if (CheckPointDistanceEstimate < nbytes)
6850		CheckPointDistanceEstimate = nbytes;
6851	else
6852		CheckPointDistanceEstimate =
6853			(0.90 * CheckPointDistanceEstimate + 0.10 * (double) nbytes);
6854}
6855
6856/*
6857 * Update the ps display for a process running a checkpoint.  Note that
6858 * this routine should not do any allocations so as it can be called
6859 * from a critical section.
6860 */
6861static void
6862update_checkpoint_display(int flags, bool restartpoint, bool reset)
6863{
6864	/*
6865	 * The status is reported only for end-of-recovery and shutdown
6866	 * checkpoints or shutdown restartpoints.  Updating the ps display is
6867	 * useful in those situations as it may not be possible to rely on
6868	 * pg_stat_activity to see the status of the checkpointer or the startup
6869	 * process.
6870	 */
6871	if ((flags & (CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY | CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN)) == 0)
6872		return;
6873
6874	if (reset)
6875		set_ps_display("");
6876	else
6877	{
6878		char		activitymsg[128];
6879
6880		snprintf(activitymsg, sizeof(activitymsg), "performing %s%s%s",
6881				 (flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY) ? "end-of-recovery " : "",
6882				 (flags & CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN) ? "shutdown " : "",
6883				 restartpoint ? "restartpoint" : "checkpoint");
6884		set_ps_display(activitymsg);
6885	}
6886}
6887
6888
6889/*
6890 * Perform a checkpoint --- either during shutdown, or on-the-fly
6891 *
6892 * flags is a bitwise OR of the following:
6893 *	CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN: checkpoint is for database shutdown.
6894 *	CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY: checkpoint is for end of WAL recovery.
6895 *	CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE: finish the checkpoint ASAP,
6896 *		ignoring checkpoint_completion_target parameter.
6897 *	CHECKPOINT_FORCE: force a checkpoint even if no XLOG activity has occurred
6898 *		since the last one (implied by CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN or
6899 *		CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY).
6900 *	CHECKPOINT_FLUSH_ALL: also flush buffers of unlogged tables.
6901 *
6902 * Note: flags contains other bits, of interest here only for logging purposes.
6903 * In particular note that this routine is synchronous and does not pay
6904 * attention to CHECKPOINT_WAIT.
6905 *
6906 * If !shutdown then we are writing an online checkpoint. An XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO
6907 * record is inserted into WAL at the logical location of the checkpoint, before
6908 * flushing anything to disk, and when the checkpoint is eventually completed,
6909 * and it is from this point that WAL replay will begin in the case of a recovery
6910 * from this checkpoint. Once everything is written to disk, an
6911 * XLOG_CHECKPOINT_ONLINE record is written to complete the checkpoint, and
6912 * points back to the earlier XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO record. This mechanism allows
6913 * other write-ahead log records to be written while the checkpoint is in
6914 * progress, but we must be very careful about order of operations. This function
6915 * may take many minutes to execute on a busy system.
6916 *
6917 * On the other hand, when shutdown is true, concurrent insertion into the
6918 * write-ahead log is impossible, so there is no need for two separate records.
6919 * In this case, we only insert an XLOG_CHECKPOINT_SHUTDOWN record, and it's
6920 * both the record marking the completion of the checkpoint and the location
6921 * from which WAL replay would begin if needed.
6922 *
6923 * Returns true if a new checkpoint was performed, or false if it was skipped
6924 * because the system was idle.
6925 */
6926bool
6927CreateCheckPoint(int flags)
6928{
6929	bool		shutdown;
6930	CheckPoint	checkPoint;
6931	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
6932	XLogSegNo	_logSegNo;
6933	XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
6934	uint32		freespace;
6935	XLogRecPtr	PriorRedoPtr;
6936	XLogRecPtr	last_important_lsn;
6937	VirtualTransactionId *vxids;
6938	int			nvxids;
6939	int			oldXLogAllowed = 0;
6940
6941	/*
6942	 * An end-of-recovery checkpoint is really a shutdown checkpoint, just
6943	 * issued at a different time.
6944	 */
6945	if (flags & (CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN | CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY))
6946		shutdown = true;
6947	else
6948		shutdown = false;
6949
6950	/* sanity check */
6951	if (RecoveryInProgress() && (flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY) == 0)
6952		elog(ERROR, "can't create a checkpoint during recovery");
6953
6954	/*
6955	 * Prepare to accumulate statistics.
6956	 *
6957	 * Note: because it is possible for log_checkpoints to change while a
6958	 * checkpoint proceeds, we always accumulate stats, even if
6959	 * log_checkpoints is currently off.
6960	 */
6961	MemSet(&CheckpointStats, 0, sizeof(CheckpointStats));
6962	CheckpointStats.ckpt_start_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
6963
6964	/*
6965	 * Let smgr prepare for checkpoint; this has to happen outside the
6966	 * critical section and before we determine the REDO pointer.  Note that
6967	 * smgr must not do anything that'd have to be undone if we decide no
6968	 * checkpoint is needed.
6969	 */
6970	SyncPreCheckpoint();
6971
6972	/*
6973	 * Use a critical section to force system panic if we have trouble.
6974	 */
6975	START_CRIT_SECTION();
6976
6977	if (shutdown)
6978	{
6979		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
6980		ControlFile->state = DB_SHUTDOWNING;
6981		UpdateControlFile();
6982		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
6983	}
6984
6985	/* Begin filling in the checkpoint WAL record */
6986	MemSet(&checkPoint, 0, sizeof(checkPoint));
6987	checkPoint.time = (pg_time_t) time(NULL);
6988
6989	/*
6990	 * For Hot Standby, derive the oldestActiveXid before we fix the redo
6991	 * pointer. This allows us to begin accumulating changes to assemble our
6992	 * starting snapshot of locks and transactions.
6993	 */
6994	if (!shutdown && XLogStandbyInfoActive())
6995		checkPoint.oldestActiveXid = GetOldestActiveTransactionId();
6996	else
6997		checkPoint.oldestActiveXid = InvalidTransactionId;
6998
6999	/*
7000	 * Get location of last important record before acquiring insert locks (as
7001	 * GetLastImportantRecPtr() also locks WAL locks).
7002	 */
7003	last_important_lsn = GetLastImportantRecPtr();
7004
7005	/*
7006	 * If this isn't a shutdown or forced checkpoint, and if there has been no
7007	 * WAL activity requiring a checkpoint, skip it.  The idea here is to
7008	 * avoid inserting duplicate checkpoints when the system is idle.
7009	 */
7010	if ((flags & (CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN | CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY |
7011				  CHECKPOINT_FORCE)) == 0)
7012	{
7013		if (last_important_lsn == ControlFile->checkPoint)
7014		{
7015			END_CRIT_SECTION();
7016			ereport(DEBUG1,
7017					(errmsg_internal("checkpoint skipped because system is idle")));
7018			return false;
7019		}
7020	}
7021
7022	/*
7023	 * An end-of-recovery checkpoint is created before anyone is allowed to
7024	 * write WAL. To allow us to write the checkpoint record, temporarily
7025	 * enable XLogInsertAllowed.
7026	 */
7027	if (flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY)
7028		oldXLogAllowed = LocalSetXLogInsertAllowed();
7029
7030	checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID = XLogCtl->InsertTimeLineID;
7031	if (flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY)
7032		checkPoint.PrevTimeLineID = XLogCtl->PrevTimeLineID;
7033	else
7034		checkPoint.PrevTimeLineID = checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID;
7035
7036	/*
7037	 * We must block concurrent insertions while examining insert state.
7038	 */
7039	WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
7040
7041	checkPoint.fullPageWrites = Insert->fullPageWrites;
7042	checkPoint.wal_level = wal_level;
7043
7044	if (shutdown)
7045	{
7046		XLogRecPtr	curInsert = XLogBytePosToRecPtr(Insert->CurrBytePos);
7047
7048		/*
7049		 * Compute new REDO record ptr = location of next XLOG record.
7050		 *
7051		 * Since this is a shutdown checkpoint, there can't be any concurrent
7052		 * WAL insertion.
7053		 */
7054		freespace = INSERT_FREESPACE(curInsert);
7055		if (freespace == 0)
7056		{
7057			if (XLogSegmentOffset(curInsert, wal_segment_size) == 0)
7058				curInsert += SizeOfXLogLongPHD;
7059			else
7060				curInsert += SizeOfXLogShortPHD;
7061		}
7062		checkPoint.redo = curInsert;
7063
7064		/*
7065		 * Here we update the shared RedoRecPtr for future XLogInsert calls;
7066		 * this must be done while holding all the insertion locks.
7067		 *
7068		 * Note: if we fail to complete the checkpoint, RedoRecPtr will be
7069		 * left pointing past where it really needs to point.  This is okay;
7070		 * the only consequence is that XLogInsert might back up whole buffers
7071		 * that it didn't really need to.  We can't postpone advancing
7072		 * RedoRecPtr because XLogInserts that happen while we are dumping
7073		 * buffers must assume that their buffer changes are not included in
7074		 * the checkpoint.
7075		 */
7076		RedoRecPtr = XLogCtl->Insert.RedoRecPtr = checkPoint.redo;
7077	}
7078
7079	/*
7080	 * Now we can release the WAL insertion locks, allowing other xacts to
7081	 * proceed while we are flushing disk buffers.
7082	 */
7083	WALInsertLockRelease();
7084
7085	/*
7086	 * If this is an online checkpoint, we have not yet determined the redo
7087	 * point. We do so now by inserting the special XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO
7088	 * record; the LSN at which it starts becomes the new redo pointer. We
7089	 * don't do this for a shutdown checkpoint, because in that case no WAL
7090	 * can be written between the redo point and the insertion of the
7091	 * checkpoint record itself, so the checkpoint record itself serves to
7092	 * mark the redo point.
7093	 */
7094	if (!shutdown)
7095	{
7096		/* Include WAL level in record for WAL summarizer's benefit. */
7097		XLogBeginInsert();
7098		XLogRegisterData(&wal_level, sizeof(wal_level));
7099		(void) XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO);
7100
7101		/*
7102		 * XLogInsertRecord will have updated XLogCtl->Insert.RedoRecPtr in
7103		 * shared memory and RedoRecPtr in backend-local memory, but we need
7104		 * to copy that into the record that will be inserted when the
7105		 * checkpoint is complete.
7106		 */
7107		checkPoint.redo = RedoRecPtr;
7108	}
7109
7110	/* Update the info_lck-protected copy of RedoRecPtr as well */
7111	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7112	XLogCtl->RedoRecPtr = checkPoint.redo;
7113	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7114
7115	/*
7116	 * If enabled, log checkpoint start.  We postpone this until now so as not
7117	 * to log anything if we decided to skip the checkpoint.
7118	 */
7119	if (log_checkpoints)
7120		LogCheckpointStart(flags, false);
7121
7122	/* Update the process title */
7123	update_checkpoint_display(flags, false, false);
7124
7125	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_CHECKPOINT_START(flags);
7126
7127	/*
7128	 * Get the other info we need for the checkpoint record.
7129	 *
7130	 * We don't need to save oldestClogXid in the checkpoint, it only matters
7131	 * for the short period in which clog is being truncated, and if we crash
7132	 * during that we'll redo the clog truncation and fix up oldestClogXid
7133	 * there.
7134	 */
7135	LWLockAcquire(XidGenLock, LW_SHARED);
7136	checkPoint.nextXid = TransamVariables->nextXid;
7137	checkPoint.oldestXid = TransamVariables->oldestXid;
7138	checkPoint.oldestXidDB = TransamVariables->oldestXidDB;
7139	LWLockRelease(XidGenLock);
7140
7141	LWLockAcquire(CommitTsLock, LW_SHARED);
7142	checkPoint.oldestCommitTsXid = TransamVariables->oldestCommitTsXid;
7143	checkPoint.newestCommitTsXid = TransamVariables->newestCommitTsXid;
7144	LWLockRelease(CommitTsLock);
7145
7146	LWLockAcquire(OidGenLock, LW_SHARED);
7147	checkPoint.nextOid = TransamVariables->nextOid;
7148	if (!shutdown)
7149		checkPoint.nextOid += TransamVariables->oidCount;
7150	LWLockRelease(OidGenLock);
7151
7152	MultiXactGetCheckptMulti(shutdown,
7153							 &checkPoint.nextMulti,
7154							 &checkPoint.nextMultiOffset,
7155							 &checkPoint.oldestMulti,
7156							 &checkPoint.oldestMultiDB);
7157
7158	/*
7159	 * Having constructed the checkpoint record, ensure all shmem disk buffers
7160	 * and commit-log buffers are flushed to disk.
7161	 *
7162	 * This I/O could fail for various reasons.  If so, we will fail to
7163	 * complete the checkpoint, but there is no reason to force a system
7164	 * panic. Accordingly, exit critical section while doing it.
7165	 */
7166	END_CRIT_SECTION();
7167
7168	/*
7169	 * In some cases there are groups of actions that must all occur on one
7170	 * side or the other of a checkpoint record. Before flushing the
7171	 * checkpoint record we must explicitly wait for any backend currently
7172	 * performing those groups of actions.
7173	 *
7174	 * One example is end of transaction, so we must wait for any transactions
7175	 * that are currently in commit critical sections.  If an xact inserted
7176	 * its commit record into XLOG just before the REDO point, then a crash
7177	 * restart from the REDO point would not replay that record, which means
7178	 * that our flushing had better include the xact's update of pg_xact.  So
7179	 * we wait till he's out of his commit critical section before proceeding.
7180	 * See notes in RecordTransactionCommit().
7181	 *
7182	 * Because we've already released the insertion locks, this test is a bit
7183	 * fuzzy: it is possible that we will wait for xacts we didn't really need
7184	 * to wait for.  But the delay should be short and it seems better to make
7185	 * checkpoint take a bit longer than to hold off insertions longer than
7186	 * necessary. (In fact, the whole reason we have this issue is that xact.c
7187	 * does commit record XLOG insertion and clog update as two separate steps
7188	 * protected by different locks, but again that seems best on grounds of
7189	 * minimizing lock contention.)
7190	 *
7191	 * A transaction that has not yet set delayChkptFlags when we look cannot
7192	 * be at risk, since it has not inserted its commit record yet; and one
7193	 * that's already cleared it is not at risk either, since it's done fixing
7194	 * clog and we will correctly flush the update below.  So we cannot miss
7195	 * any xacts we need to wait for.
7196	 */
7197	vxids = GetVirtualXIDsDelayingChkpt(&nvxids, DELAY_CHKPT_START);
7198	if (nvxids > 0)
7199	{
7200		do
7201		{
7202			/*
7203			 * Keep absorbing fsync requests while we wait. There could even
7204			 * be a deadlock if we don't, if the process that prevents the
7205			 * checkpoint is trying to add a request to the queue.
7206			 */
7207			AbsorbSyncRequests();
7208
7209			pgstat_report_wait_start(WAIT_EVENT_CHECKPOINT_DELAY_START);
7210			pg_usleep(10000L);	/* wait for 10 msec */
7211			pgstat_report_wait_end();
7212		} while (HaveVirtualXIDsDelayingChkpt(vxids, nvxids,
7213											  DELAY_CHKPT_START));
7214	}
7215	pfree(vxids);
7216
7217	CheckPointGuts(checkPoint.redo, flags);
7218
7219	vxids = GetVirtualXIDsDelayingChkpt(&nvxids, DELAY_CHKPT_COMPLETE);
7220	if (nvxids > 0)
7221	{
7222		do
7223		{
7224			AbsorbSyncRequests();
7225
7226			pgstat_report_wait_start(WAIT_EVENT_CHECKPOINT_DELAY_COMPLETE);
7227			pg_usleep(10000L);	/* wait for 10 msec */
7228			pgstat_report_wait_end();
7229		} while (HaveVirtualXIDsDelayingChkpt(vxids, nvxids,
7230											  DELAY_CHKPT_COMPLETE));
7231	}
7232	pfree(vxids);
7233
7234	/*
7235	 * Take a snapshot of running transactions and write this to WAL. This
7236	 * allows us to reconstruct the state of running transactions during
7237	 * archive recovery, if required. Skip, if this info disabled.
7238	 *
7239	 * If we are shutting down, or Startup process is completing crash
7240	 * recovery we don't need to write running xact data.
7241	 */
7242	if (!shutdown && XLogStandbyInfoActive())
7243		LogStandbySnapshot();
7244
7245	START_CRIT_SECTION();
7246
7247	/*
7248	 * Now insert the checkpoint record into XLOG.
7249	 */
7250	XLogBeginInsert();
7251	XLogRegisterData(&checkPoint, sizeof(checkPoint));
7252	recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID,
7253						shutdown ? XLOG_CHECKPOINT_SHUTDOWN :
7254						XLOG_CHECKPOINT_ONLINE);
7255
7256	XLogFlush(recptr);
7257
7258	/*
7259	 * We mustn't write any new WAL after a shutdown checkpoint, or it will be
7260	 * overwritten at next startup.  No-one should even try, this just allows
7261	 * sanity-checking.  In the case of an end-of-recovery checkpoint, we want
7262	 * to just temporarily disable writing until the system has exited
7263	 * recovery.
7264	 */
7265	if (shutdown)
7266	{
7267		if (flags & CHECKPOINT_END_OF_RECOVERY)
7268			LocalXLogInsertAllowed = oldXLogAllowed;
7269		else
7270			LocalXLogInsertAllowed = 0; /* never again write WAL */
7271	}
7272
7273	/*
7274	 * We now have ProcLastRecPtr = start of actual checkpoint record, recptr
7275	 * = end of actual checkpoint record.
7276	 */
7277	if (shutdown && checkPoint.redo != ProcLastRecPtr)
7278		ereport(PANIC,
7279				(errmsg("concurrent write-ahead log activity while database system is shutting down")));
7280
7281	/*
7282	 * Remember the prior checkpoint's redo ptr for
7283	 * UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate()
7284	 */
7285	PriorRedoPtr = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo;
7286
7287	/*
7288	 * Update the control file.
7289	 */
7290	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
7291	if (shutdown)
7292		ControlFile->state = DB_SHUTDOWNED;
7293	ControlFile->checkPoint = ProcLastRecPtr;
7294	ControlFile->checkPointCopy = checkPoint;
7295	/* crash recovery should always recover to the end of WAL */
7296	ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint = InvalidXLogRecPtr;
7297	ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI = 0;
7298
7299	/*
7300	 * Persist unloggedLSN value. It's reset on crash recovery, so this goes
7301	 * unused on non-shutdown checkpoints, but seems useful to store it always
7302	 * for debugging purposes.
7303	 */
7304	ControlFile->unloggedLSN = pg_atomic_read_membarrier_u64(&XLogCtl->unloggedLSN);
7305
7306	UpdateControlFile();
7307	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
7308
7309	/* Update shared-memory copy of checkpoint XID/epoch */
7310	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7311	XLogCtl->ckptFullXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
7312	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7313
7314	/*
7315	 * We are now done with critical updates; no need for system panic if we
7316	 * have trouble while fooling with old log segments.
7317	 */
7318	END_CRIT_SECTION();
7319
7320	/*
7321	 * WAL summaries end when the next XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO or
7322	 * XLOG_CHECKPOINT_SHUTDOWN record is reached. This is the first point
7323	 * where (a) we're not inside of a critical section and (b) we can be
7324	 * certain that the relevant record has been flushed to disk, which must
7325	 * happen before it can be summarized.
7326	 *
7327	 * If this is a shutdown checkpoint, then this happens reasonably
7328	 * promptly: we've only just inserted and flushed the
7329	 * XLOG_CHECKPOINT_SHUTDOWN record. If this is not a shutdown checkpoint,
7330	 * then this might not be very prompt at all: the XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO
7331	 * record was written before we began flushing data to disk, and that
7332	 * could be many minutes ago at this point. However, we don't XLogFlush()
7333	 * after inserting that record, so we're not guaranteed that it's on disk
7334	 * until after the above call that flushes the XLOG_CHECKPOINT_ONLINE
7335	 * record.
7336	 */
7337	WakeupWalSummarizer();
7338
7339	/*
7340	 * Let smgr do post-checkpoint cleanup (eg, deleting old files).
7341	 */
7342	SyncPostCheckpoint();
7343
7344	/*
7345	 * Update the average distance between checkpoints if the prior checkpoint
7346	 * exists.
7347	 */
7348	if (PriorRedoPtr != InvalidXLogRecPtr)
7349		UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate(RedoRecPtr - PriorRedoPtr);
7350
7351	INJECTION_POINT("checkpoint-before-old-wal-removal", NULL);
7352
7353	/*
7354	 * Delete old log files, those no longer needed for last checkpoint to
7355	 * prevent the disk holding the xlog from growing full.
7356	 */
7357	XLByteToSeg(RedoRecPtr, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
7358	KeepLogSeg(recptr, &_logSegNo);
7359	if (InvalidateObsoleteReplicationSlots(RS_INVAL_WAL_REMOVED | RS_INVAL_IDLE_TIMEOUT,
7360										   _logSegNo, InvalidOid,
7361										   InvalidTransactionId))
7362	{
7363		/*
7364		 * Some slots have been invalidated; recalculate the old-segment
7365		 * horizon, starting again from RedoRecPtr.
7366		 */
7367		XLByteToSeg(RedoRecPtr, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
7368		KeepLogSeg(recptr, &_logSegNo);
7369	}
7370	_logSegNo--;
7371	RemoveOldXlogFiles(_logSegNo, RedoRecPtr, recptr,
7372					   checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID);
7373
7374	/*
7375	 * Make more log segments if needed.  (Do this after recycling old log
7376	 * segments, since that may supply some of the needed files.)
7377	 */
7378	if (!shutdown)
7379		PreallocXlogFiles(recptr, checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID);
7380
7381	/*
7382	 * Truncate pg_subtrans if possible.  We can throw away all data before
7383	 * the oldest XMIN of any running transaction.  No future transaction will
7384	 * attempt to reference any pg_subtrans entry older than that (see Asserts
7385	 * in subtrans.c).  During recovery, though, we mustn't do this because
7386	 * StartupSUBTRANS hasn't been called yet.
7387	 */
7388	if (!RecoveryInProgress())
7389		TruncateSUBTRANS(GetOldestTransactionIdConsideredRunning());
7390
7391	/* Real work is done; log and update stats. */
7392	LogCheckpointEnd(false);
7393
7394	/* Reset the process title */
7395	update_checkpoint_display(flags, false, true);
7396
7397	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_CHECKPOINT_DONE(CheckpointStats.ckpt_bufs_written,
7398									 NBuffers,
7399									 CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_added,
7400									 CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_removed,
7401									 CheckpointStats.ckpt_segs_recycled);
7402
7403	return true;
7404}
7405
7406/*
7407 * Mark the end of recovery in WAL though without running a full checkpoint.
7408 * We can expect that a restartpoint is likely to be in progress as we
7409 * do this, though we are unwilling to wait for it to complete.
7410 *
7411 * CreateRestartPoint() allows for the case where recovery may end before
7412 * the restartpoint completes so there is no concern of concurrent behaviour.
7413 */
7414static void
7415CreateEndOfRecoveryRecord(void)
7416{
7417	xl_end_of_recovery xlrec;
7418	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
7419
7420	/* sanity check */
7421	if (!RecoveryInProgress())
7422		elog(ERROR, "can only be used to end recovery");
7423
7424	xlrec.end_time = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7425	xlrec.wal_level = wal_level;
7426
7427	WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
7428	xlrec.ThisTimeLineID = XLogCtl->InsertTimeLineID;
7429	xlrec.PrevTimeLineID = XLogCtl->PrevTimeLineID;
7430	WALInsertLockRelease();
7431
7432	START_CRIT_SECTION();
7433
7434	XLogBeginInsert();
7435	XLogRegisterData(&xlrec, sizeof(xl_end_of_recovery));
7436	recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_END_OF_RECOVERY);
7437
7438	XLogFlush(recptr);
7439
7440	/*
7441	 * Update the control file so that crash recovery can follow the timeline
7442	 * changes to this point.
7443	 */
7444	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
7445	ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint = recptr;
7446	ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI = xlrec.ThisTimeLineID;
7447	UpdateControlFile();
7448	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
7449
7450	END_CRIT_SECTION();
7451}
7452
7453/*
7454 * Write an OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD message.
7455 *
7456 * When on WAL replay we expect a continuation record at the start of a page
7457 * that is not there, recovery ends and WAL writing resumes at that point.
7458 * But it's wrong to resume writing new WAL back at the start of the record
7459 * that was broken, because downstream consumers of that WAL (physical
7460 * replicas) are not prepared to "rewind".  So the first action after
7461 * finishing replay of all valid WAL must be to write a record of this type
7462 * at the point where the contrecord was missing; to support xlogreader
7463 * detecting the special case, XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD is also added
7464 * to the page header where the record occurs.  xlogreader has an ad-hoc
7465 * mechanism to report metadata about the broken record, which is what we
7466 * use here.
7467 *
7468 * At replay time, XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD instructs xlogreader to
7469 * skip the record it was reading, and pass back the LSN of the skipped
7470 * record, so that its caller can verify (on "replay" of that record) that the
7471 * XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD matches what was effectively overwritten.
7472 *
7473 * 'aborted_lsn' is the beginning position of the record that was incomplete.
7474 * It is included in the WAL record.  'pagePtr' and 'newTLI' point to the
7475 * beginning of the XLOG page where the record is to be inserted.  They must
7476 * match the current WAL insert position, they're passed here just so that we
7477 * can verify that.
7478 */
7479static XLogRecPtr
7480CreateOverwriteContrecordRecord(XLogRecPtr aborted_lsn, XLogRecPtr pagePtr,
7481								TimeLineID newTLI)
7482{
7483	xl_overwrite_contrecord xlrec;
7484	XLogRecPtr	recptr;
7485	XLogPageHeader pagehdr;
7486	XLogRecPtr	startPos;
7487
7488	/* sanity checks */
7489	if (!RecoveryInProgress())
7490		elog(ERROR, "can only be used at end of recovery");
7491	if (pagePtr % XLOG_BLCKSZ != 0)
7492		elog(ERROR, "invalid position for missing continuation record %X/%X",
7493			 LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(pagePtr));
7494
7495	/* The current WAL insert position should be right after the page header */
7496	startPos = pagePtr;
7497	if (XLogSegmentOffset(startPos, wal_segment_size) == 0)
7498		startPos += SizeOfXLogLongPHD;
7499	else
7500		startPos += SizeOfXLogShortPHD;
7501	recptr = GetXLogInsertRecPtr();
7502	if (recptr != startPos)
7503		elog(ERROR, "invalid WAL insert position %X/%X for OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD",
7504			 LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(recptr));
7505
7506	START_CRIT_SECTION();
7507
7508	/*
7509	 * Initialize the XLOG page header (by GetXLogBuffer), and set the
7510	 * XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD flag.
7511	 *
7512	 * No other backend is allowed to write WAL yet, so acquiring the WAL
7513	 * insertion lock is just pro forma.
7514	 */
7515	WALInsertLockAcquire();
7516	pagehdr = (XLogPageHeader) GetXLogBuffer(pagePtr, newTLI);
7517	pagehdr->xlp_info |= XLP_FIRST_IS_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD;
7518	WALInsertLockRelease();
7519
7520	/*
7521	 * Insert the XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD record as the first record on the
7522	 * page.  We know it becomes the first record, because no other backend is
7523	 * allowed to write WAL yet.
7524	 */
7525	XLogBeginInsert();
7526	xlrec.overwritten_lsn = aborted_lsn;
7527	xlrec.overwrite_time = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7528	XLogRegisterData(&xlrec, sizeof(xl_overwrite_contrecord));
7529	recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD);
7530
7531	/* check that the record was inserted to the right place */
7532	if (ProcLastRecPtr != startPos)
7533		elog(ERROR, "OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD was inserted to unexpected position %X/%X",
7534			 LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(ProcLastRecPtr));
7535
7536	XLogFlush(recptr);
7537
7538	END_CRIT_SECTION();
7539
7540	return recptr;
7541}
7542
7543/*
7544 * Flush all data in shared memory to disk, and fsync
7545 *
7546 * This is the common code shared between regular checkpoints and
7547 * recovery restartpoints.
7548 */
7549static void
7550CheckPointGuts(XLogRecPtr checkPointRedo, int flags)
7551{
7552	CheckPointRelationMap();
7553	CheckPointReplicationSlots(flags & CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN);
7554	CheckPointSnapBuild();
7555	CheckPointLogicalRewriteHeap();
7556	CheckPointReplicationOrigin();
7557
7558	/* Write out all dirty data in SLRUs and the main buffer pool */
7559	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_CHECKPOINT_START(flags);
7560	CheckpointStats.ckpt_write_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7561	CheckPointCLOG();
7562	CheckPointCommitTs();
7563	CheckPointSUBTRANS();
7564	CheckPointMultiXact();
7565	CheckPointPredicate();
7566	CheckPointBuffers(flags);
7567
7568	/* Perform all queued up fsyncs */
7569	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_CHECKPOINT_SYNC_START();
7570	CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7571	ProcessSyncRequests();
7572	CheckpointStats.ckpt_sync_end_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7573	TRACE_POSTGRESQL_BUFFER_CHECKPOINT_DONE();
7574
7575	/* We deliberately delay 2PC checkpointing as long as possible */
7576	CheckPointTwoPhase(checkPointRedo);
7577}
7578
7579/*
7580 * Save a checkpoint for recovery restart if appropriate
7581 *
7582 * This function is called each time a checkpoint record is read from XLOG.
7583 * It must determine whether the checkpoint represents a safe restartpoint or
7584 * not.  If so, the checkpoint record is stashed in shared memory so that
7585 * CreateRestartPoint can consult it.  (Note that the latter function is
7586 * executed by the checkpointer, while this one will be executed by the
7587 * startup process.)
7588 */
7589static void
7590RecoveryRestartPoint(const CheckPoint *checkPoint, XLogReaderState *record)
7591{
7592	/*
7593	 * Also refrain from creating a restartpoint if we have seen any
7594	 * references to non-existent pages. Restarting recovery from the
7595	 * restartpoint would not see the references, so we would lose the
7596	 * cross-check that the pages belonged to a relation that was dropped
7597	 * later.
7598	 */
7599	if (XLogHaveInvalidPages())
7600	{
7601		elog(DEBUG2,
7602			 "could not record restart point at %X/%X because there "
7603			 "are unresolved references to invalid pages",
7604			 LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(checkPoint->redo));
7605		return;
7606	}
7607
7608	/*
7609	 * Copy the checkpoint record to shared memory, so that checkpointer can
7610	 * work out the next time it wants to perform a restartpoint.
7611	 */
7612	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7613	XLogCtl->lastCheckPointRecPtr = record->ReadRecPtr;
7614	XLogCtl->lastCheckPointEndPtr = record->EndRecPtr;
7615	XLogCtl->lastCheckPoint = *checkPoint;
7616	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7617}
7618
7619/*
7620 * Establish a restartpoint if possible.
7621 *
7622 * This is similar to CreateCheckPoint, but is used during WAL recovery
7623 * to establish a point from which recovery can roll forward without
7624 * replaying the entire recovery log.
7625 *
7626 * Returns true if a new restartpoint was established. We can only establish
7627 * a restartpoint if we have replayed a safe checkpoint record since last
7628 * restartpoint.
7629 */
7630bool
7631CreateRestartPoint(int flags)
7632{
7633	XLogRecPtr	lastCheckPointRecPtr;
7634	XLogRecPtr	lastCheckPointEndPtr;
7635	CheckPoint	lastCheckPoint;
7636	XLogRecPtr	PriorRedoPtr;
7637	XLogRecPtr	receivePtr;
7638	XLogRecPtr	replayPtr;
7639	TimeLineID	replayTLI;
7640	XLogRecPtr	endptr;
7641	XLogSegNo	_logSegNo;
7642	TimestampTz xtime;
7643
7644	/* Concurrent checkpoint/restartpoint cannot happen */
7645	Assert(!IsUnderPostmaster || MyBackendType == B_CHECKPOINTER);
7646
7647	/* Get a local copy of the last safe checkpoint record. */
7648	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7649	lastCheckPointRecPtr = XLogCtl->lastCheckPointRecPtr;
7650	lastCheckPointEndPtr = XLogCtl->lastCheckPointEndPtr;
7651	lastCheckPoint = XLogCtl->lastCheckPoint;
7652	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7653
7654	/*
7655	 * Check that we're still in recovery mode. It's ok if we exit recovery
7656	 * mode after this check, the restart point is valid anyway.
7657	 */
7658	if (!RecoveryInProgress())
7659	{
7660		ereport(DEBUG2,
7661				(errmsg_internal("skipping restartpoint, recovery has already ended")));
7662		return false;
7663	}
7664
7665	/*
7666	 * If the last checkpoint record we've replayed is already our last
7667	 * restartpoint, we can't perform a new restart point. We still update
7668	 * minRecoveryPoint in that case, so that if this is a shutdown restart
7669	 * point, we won't start up earlier than before. That's not strictly
7670	 * necessary, but when hot standby is enabled, it would be rather weird if
7671	 * the database opened up for read-only connections at a point-in-time
7672	 * before the last shutdown. Such time travel is still possible in case of
7673	 * immediate shutdown, though.
7674	 *
7675	 * We don't explicitly advance minRecoveryPoint when we do create a
7676	 * restartpoint. It's assumed that flushing the buffers will do that as a
7677	 * side-effect.
7678	 */
7679	if (XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(lastCheckPointRecPtr) ||
7680		lastCheckPoint.redo <= ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo)
7681	{
7682		ereport(DEBUG2,
7683				(errmsg_internal("skipping restartpoint, already performed at %X/%X",
7684								 LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(lastCheckPoint.redo))));
7685
7686		UpdateMinRecoveryPoint(InvalidXLogRecPtr, true);
7687		if (flags & CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN)
7688		{
7689			LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
7690			ControlFile->state = DB_SHUTDOWNED_IN_RECOVERY;
7691			UpdateControlFile();
7692			LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
7693		}
7694		return false;
7695	}
7696
7697	/*
7698	 * Update the shared RedoRecPtr so that the startup process can calculate
7699	 * the number of segments replayed since last restartpoint, and request a
7700	 * restartpoint if it exceeds CheckPointSegments.
7701	 *
7702	 * Like in CreateCheckPoint(), hold off insertions to update it, although
7703	 * during recovery this is just pro forma, because no WAL insertions are
7704	 * happening.
7705	 */
7706	WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
7707	RedoRecPtr = XLogCtl->Insert.RedoRecPtr = lastCheckPoint.redo;
7708	WALInsertLockRelease();
7709
7710	/* Also update the info_lck-protected copy */
7711	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7712	XLogCtl->RedoRecPtr = lastCheckPoint.redo;
7713	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
7714
7715	/*
7716	 * Prepare to accumulate statistics.
7717	 *
7718	 * Note: because it is possible for log_checkpoints to change while a
7719	 * checkpoint proceeds, we always accumulate stats, even if
7720	 * log_checkpoints is currently off.
7721	 */
7722	MemSet(&CheckpointStats, 0, sizeof(CheckpointStats));
7723	CheckpointStats.ckpt_start_t = GetCurrentTimestamp();
7724
7725	if (log_checkpoints)
7726		LogCheckpointStart(flags, true);
7727
7728	/* Update the process title */
7729	update_checkpoint_display(flags, true, false);
7730
7731	CheckPointGuts(lastCheckPoint.redo, flags);
7732
7733	/*
7734	 * This location needs to be after CheckPointGuts() to ensure that some
7735	 * work has already happened during this checkpoint.
7736	 */
7737	INJECTION_POINT("create-restart-point", NULL);
7738
7739	/*
7740	 * Remember the prior checkpoint's redo ptr for
7741	 * UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate()
7742	 */
7743	PriorRedoPtr = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo;
7744
7745	/*
7746	 * Update pg_control, using current time.  Check that it still shows an
7747	 * older checkpoint, else do nothing; this is a quick hack to make sure
7748	 * nothing really bad happens if somehow we get here after the
7749	 * end-of-recovery checkpoint.
7750	 */
7751	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
7752	if (ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo < lastCheckPoint.redo)
7753	{
7754		/*
7755		 * Update the checkpoint information.  We do this even if the cluster
7756		 * does not show DB_IN_ARCHIVE_RECOVERY to match with the set of WAL
7757		 * segments recycled below.
7758		 */
7759		ControlFile->checkPoint = lastCheckPointRecPtr;
7760		ControlFile->checkPointCopy = lastCheckPoint;
7761
7762		/*
7763		 * Ensure minRecoveryPoint is past the checkpoint record and update it
7764		 * if the control file still shows DB_IN_ARCHIVE_RECOVERY.  Normally,
7765		 * this will have happened already while writing out dirty buffers,
7766		 * but not necessarily - e.g. because no buffers were dirtied.  We do
7767		 * this because a backup performed in recovery uses minRecoveryPoint
7768		 * to determine which WAL files must be included in the backup, and
7769		 * the file (or files) containing the checkpoint record must be
7770		 * included, at a minimum.  Note that for an ordinary restart of
7771		 * recovery there's no value in having the minimum recovery point any
7772		 * earlier than this anyway, because redo will begin just after the
7773		 * checkpoint record.
7774		 */
7775		if (ControlFile->state == DB_IN_ARCHIVE_RECOVERY)
7776		{
7777			if (ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint < lastCheckPointEndPtr)
7778			{
7779				ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint = lastCheckPointEndPtr;
7780				ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI = lastCheckPoint.ThisTimeLineID;
7781
7782				/* update local copy */
7783				LocalMinRecoveryPoint = ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint;
7784				LocalMinRecoveryPointTLI = ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI;
7785			}
7786			if (flags & CHECKPOINT_IS_SHUTDOWN)
7787				ControlFile->state = DB_SHUTDOWNED_IN_RECOVERY;
7788		}
7789		UpdateControlFile();
7790	}
7791	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
7792
7793	/*
7794	 * Update the average distance between checkpoints/restartpoints if the
7795	 * prior checkpoint exists.
7796	 */
7797	if (PriorRedoPtr != InvalidXLogRecPtr)
7798		UpdateCheckPointDistanceEstimate(RedoRecPtr - PriorRedoPtr);
7799
7800	/*
7801	 * Delete old log files, those no longer needed for last restartpoint to
7802	 * prevent the disk holding the xlog from growing full.
7803	 */
7804	XLByteToSeg(RedoRecPtr, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
7805
7806	/*
7807	 * Retreat _logSegNo using the current end of xlog replayed or received,
7808	 * whichever is later.
7809	 */
7810	receivePtr = GetWalRcvFlushRecPtr(NULL, NULL);
7811	replayPtr = GetXLogReplayRecPtr(&replayTLI);
7812	endptr = (receivePtr < replayPtr) ? replayPtr : receivePtr;
7813	KeepLogSeg(endptr, &_logSegNo);
7814
7815	INJECTION_POINT("restartpoint-before-slot-invalidation", NULL);
7816
7817	if (InvalidateObsoleteReplicationSlots(RS_INVAL_WAL_REMOVED | RS_INVAL_IDLE_TIMEOUT,
7818										   _logSegNo, InvalidOid,
7819										   InvalidTransactionId))
7820	{
7821		/*
7822		 * Some slots have been invalidated; recalculate the old-segment
7823		 * horizon, starting again from RedoRecPtr.
7824		 */
7825		XLByteToSeg(RedoRecPtr, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
7826		KeepLogSeg(endptr, &_logSegNo);
7827	}
7828	_logSegNo--;
7829
7830	/*
7831	 * Try to recycle segments on a useful timeline. If we've been promoted
7832	 * since the beginning of this restartpoint, use the new timeline chosen
7833	 * at end of recovery.  If we're still in recovery, use the timeline we're
7834	 * currently replaying.
7835	 *
7836	 * There is no guarantee that the WAL segments will be useful on the
7837	 * current timeline; if recovery proceeds to a new timeline right after
7838	 * this, the pre-allocated WAL segments on this timeline will not be used,
7839	 * and will go wasted until recycled on the next restartpoint. We'll live
7840	 * with that.
7841	 */
7842	if (!RecoveryInProgress())
7843		replayTLI = XLogCtl->InsertTimeLineID;
7844
7845	RemoveOldXlogFiles(_logSegNo, RedoRecPtr, endptr, replayTLI);
7846
7847	/*
7848	 * Make more log segments if needed.  (Do this after recycling old log
7849	 * segments, since that may supply some of the needed files.)
7850	 */
7851	PreallocXlogFiles(endptr, replayTLI);
7852
7853	/*
7854	 * Truncate pg_subtrans if possible.  We can throw away all data before
7855	 * the oldest XMIN of any running transaction.  No future transaction will
7856	 * attempt to reference any pg_subtrans entry older than that (see Asserts
7857	 * in subtrans.c).  When hot standby is disabled, though, we mustn't do
7858	 * this because StartupSUBTRANS hasn't been called yet.
7859	 */
7860	if (EnableHotStandby)
7861		TruncateSUBTRANS(GetOldestTransactionIdConsideredRunning());
7862
7863	/* Real work is done; log and update stats. */
7864	LogCheckpointEnd(true);
7865
7866	/* Reset the process title */
7867	update_checkpoint_display(flags, true, true);
7868
7869	xtime = GetLatestXTime();
7870	ereport((log_checkpoints ? LOG : DEBUG2),
7871			(errmsg("recovery restart point at %X/%X",
7872					LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(lastCheckPoint.redo)),
7873			 xtime ? errdetail("Last completed transaction was at log time %s.",
7874							   timestamptz_to_str(xtime)) : 0));
7875
7876	/*
7877	 * Finally, execute archive_cleanup_command, if any.
7878	 */
7879	if (archiveCleanupCommand && strcmp(archiveCleanupCommand, "") != 0)
7880		ExecuteRecoveryCommand(archiveCleanupCommand,
7881							   "archive_cleanup_command",
7882							   false,
7883							   WAIT_EVENT_ARCHIVE_CLEANUP_COMMAND);
7884
7885	return true;
7886}
7887
7888/*
7889 * Report availability of WAL for the given target LSN
7890 *		(typically a slot's restart_lsn)
7891 *
7892 * Returns one of the following enum values:
7893 *
7894 * * WALAVAIL_RESERVED means targetLSN is available and it is in the range of
7895 *   max_wal_size.
7896 *
7897 * * WALAVAIL_EXTENDED means it is still available by preserving extra
7898 *   segments beyond max_wal_size. If max_slot_wal_keep_size is smaller
7899 *   than max_wal_size, this state is not returned.
7900 *
7901 * * WALAVAIL_UNRESERVED means it is being lost and the next checkpoint will
7902 *   remove reserved segments. The walsender using this slot may return to the
7903 *   above.
7904 *
7905 * * WALAVAIL_REMOVED means it has been removed. A replication stream on
7906 *   a slot with this LSN cannot continue.  (Any associated walsender
7907 *   processes should have been terminated already.)
7908 *
7909 * * WALAVAIL_INVALID_LSN means the slot hasn't been set to reserve WAL.
7910 */
7911WALAvailability
7912GetWALAvailability(XLogRecPtr targetLSN)
7913{
7914	XLogRecPtr	currpos;		/* current write LSN */
7915	XLogSegNo	currSeg;		/* segid of currpos */
7916	XLogSegNo	targetSeg;		/* segid of targetLSN */
7917	XLogSegNo	oldestSeg;		/* actual oldest segid */
7918	XLogSegNo	oldestSegMaxWalSize;	/* oldest segid kept by max_wal_size */
7919	XLogSegNo	oldestSlotSeg;	/* oldest segid kept by slot */
7920	uint64		keepSegs;
7921
7922	/*
7923	 * slot does not reserve WAL. Either deactivated, or has never been active
7924	 */
7925	if (XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(targetLSN))
7926		return WALAVAIL_INVALID_LSN;
7927
7928	/*
7929	 * Calculate the oldest segment currently reserved by all slots,
7930	 * considering wal_keep_size and max_slot_wal_keep_size.  Initialize
7931	 * oldestSlotSeg to the current segment.
7932	 */
7933	currpos = GetXLogWriteRecPtr();
7934	XLByteToSeg(currpos, oldestSlotSeg, wal_segment_size);
7935	KeepLogSeg(currpos, &oldestSlotSeg);
7936
7937	/*
7938	 * Find the oldest extant segment file. We get 1 until checkpoint removes
7939	 * the first WAL segment file since startup, which causes the status being
7940	 * wrong under certain abnormal conditions but that doesn't actually harm.
7941	 */
7942	oldestSeg = XLogGetLastRemovedSegno() + 1;
7943
7944	/* calculate oldest segment by max_wal_size */
7945	XLByteToSeg(currpos, currSeg, wal_segment_size);
7946	keepSegs = ConvertToXSegs(max_wal_size_mb, wal_segment_size) + 1;
7947
7948	if (currSeg > keepSegs)
7949		oldestSegMaxWalSize = currSeg - keepSegs;
7950	else
7951		oldestSegMaxWalSize = 1;
7952
7953	/* the segment we care about */
7954	XLByteToSeg(targetLSN, targetSeg, wal_segment_size);
7955
7956	/*
7957	 * No point in returning reserved or extended status values if the
7958	 * targetSeg is known to be lost.
7959	 */
7960	if (targetSeg >= oldestSlotSeg)
7961	{
7962		/* show "reserved" when targetSeg is within max_wal_size */
7963		if (targetSeg >= oldestSegMaxWalSize)
7964			return WALAVAIL_RESERVED;
7965
7966		/* being retained by slots exceeding max_wal_size */
7967		return WALAVAIL_EXTENDED;
7968	}
7969
7970	/* WAL segments are no longer retained but haven't been removed yet */
7971	if (targetSeg >= oldestSeg)
7972		return WALAVAIL_UNRESERVED;
7973
7974	/* Definitely lost */
7975	return WALAVAIL_REMOVED;
7976}
7977
7978
7979/*
7980 * Retreat *logSegNo to the last segment that we need to retain because of
7981 * either wal_keep_size or replication slots.
7982 *
7983 * This is calculated by subtracting wal_keep_size from the given xlog
7984 * location, recptr and by making sure that that result is below the
7985 * requirement of replication slots.  For the latter criterion we do consider
7986 * the effects of max_slot_wal_keep_size: reserve at most that much space back
7987 * from recptr.
7988 *
7989 * Note about replication slots: if this function calculates a value
7990 * that's further ahead than what slots need reserved, then affected
7991 * slots need to be invalidated and this function invoked again.
7992 * XXX it might be a good idea to rewrite this function so that
7993 * invalidation is optionally done here, instead.
7994 */
7995static void
7996KeepLogSeg(XLogRecPtr recptr, XLogSegNo *logSegNo)
7997{
7998	XLogSegNo	currSegNo;
7999	XLogSegNo	segno;
8000	XLogRecPtr	keep;
8001
8002	XLByteToSeg(recptr, currSegNo, wal_segment_size);
8003	segno = currSegNo;
8004
8005	/* Calculate how many segments are kept by slots. */
8006	keep = XLogGetReplicationSlotMinimumLSN();
8007	if (keep != InvalidXLogRecPtr && keep < recptr)
8008	{
8009		XLByteToSeg(keep, segno, wal_segment_size);
8010
8011		/*
8012		 * Account for max_slot_wal_keep_size to avoid keeping more than
8013		 * configured.  However, don't do that during a binary upgrade: if
8014		 * slots were to be invalidated because of this, it would not be
8015		 * possible to preserve logical ones during the upgrade.
8016		 */
8017		if (max_slot_wal_keep_size_mb >= 0 && !IsBinaryUpgrade)
8018		{
8019			uint64		slot_keep_segs;
8020
8021			slot_keep_segs =
8022				ConvertToXSegs(max_slot_wal_keep_size_mb, wal_segment_size);
8023
8024			if (currSegNo - segno > slot_keep_segs)
8025				segno = currSegNo - slot_keep_segs;
8026		}
8027	}
8028
8029	/*
8030	 * If WAL summarization is in use, don't remove WAL that has yet to be
8031	 * summarized.
8032	 */
8033	keep = GetOldestUnsummarizedLSN(NULL, NULL);
8034	if (keep != InvalidXLogRecPtr)
8035	{
8036		XLogSegNo	unsummarized_segno;
8037
8038		XLByteToSeg(keep, unsummarized_segno, wal_segment_size);
8039		if (unsummarized_segno < segno)
8040			segno = unsummarized_segno;
8041	}
8042
8043	/* but, keep at least wal_keep_size if that's set */
8044	if (wal_keep_size_mb > 0)
8045	{
8046		uint64		keep_segs;
8047
8048		keep_segs = ConvertToXSegs(wal_keep_size_mb, wal_segment_size);
8049		if (currSegNo - segno < keep_segs)
8050		{
8051			/* avoid underflow, don't go below 1 */
8052			if (currSegNo <= keep_segs)
8053				segno = 1;
8054			else
8055				segno = currSegNo - keep_segs;
8056		}
8057	}
8058
8059	/* don't delete WAL segments newer than the calculated segment */
8060	if (segno < *logSegNo)
8061		*logSegNo = segno;
8062}
8063
8064/*
8065 * Write a NEXTOID log record
8066 */
8067void
8068XLogPutNextOid(Oid nextOid)
8069{
8070	XLogBeginInsert();
8071	XLogRegisterData(&nextOid, sizeof(Oid));
8072	(void) XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_NEXTOID);
8073
8074	/*
8075	 * We need not flush the NEXTOID record immediately, because any of the
8076	 * just-allocated OIDs could only reach disk as part of a tuple insert or
8077	 * update that would have its own XLOG record that must follow the NEXTOID
8078	 * record.  Therefore, the standard buffer LSN interlock applied to those
8079	 * records will ensure no such OID reaches disk before the NEXTOID record
8080	 * does.
8081	 *
8082	 * Note, however, that the above statement only covers state "within" the
8083	 * database.  When we use a generated OID as a file or directory name, we
8084	 * are in a sense violating the basic WAL rule, because that filesystem
8085	 * change may reach disk before the NEXTOID WAL record does.  The impact
8086	 * of this is that if a database crash occurs immediately afterward, we
8087	 * might after restart re-generate the same OID and find that it conflicts
8088	 * with the leftover file or directory.  But since for safety's sake we
8089	 * always loop until finding a nonconflicting filename, this poses no real
8090	 * problem in practice. See pgsql-hackers discussion 27-Sep-2006.
8091	 */
8092}
8093
8094/*
8095 * Write an XLOG SWITCH record.
8096 *
8097 * Here we just blindly issue an XLogInsert request for the record.
8098 * All the magic happens inside XLogInsert.
8099 *
8100 * The return value is either the end+1 address of the switch record,
8101 * or the end+1 address of the prior segment if we did not need to
8102 * write a switch record because we are already at segment start.
8103 */
8104XLogRecPtr
8105RequestXLogSwitch(bool mark_unimportant)
8106{
8107	XLogRecPtr	RecPtr;
8108
8109	/* XLOG SWITCH has no data */
8110	XLogBeginInsert();
8111
8112	if (mark_unimportant)
8113		XLogSetRecordFlags(XLOG_MARK_UNIMPORTANT);
8114	RecPtr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_SWITCH);
8115
8116	return RecPtr;
8117}
8118
8119/*
8120 * Write a RESTORE POINT record
8121 */
8122XLogRecPtr
8123XLogRestorePoint(const char *rpName)
8124{
8125	XLogRecPtr	RecPtr;
8126	xl_restore_point xlrec;
8127
8128	xlrec.rp_time = GetCurrentTimestamp();
8129	strlcpy(xlrec.rp_name, rpName, MAXFNAMELEN);
8130
8131	XLogBeginInsert();
8132	XLogRegisterData(&xlrec, sizeof(xl_restore_point));
8133
8134	RecPtr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_RESTORE_POINT);
8135
8136	ereport(LOG,
8137			(errmsg("restore point \"%s\" created at %X/%X",
8138					rpName, LSN_FORMAT_ARGS(RecPtr))));
8139
8140	return RecPtr;
8141}
8142
8143/*
8144 * Check if any of the GUC parameters that are critical for hot standby
8145 * have changed, and update the value in pg_control file if necessary.
8146 */
8147static void
8148XLogReportParameters(void)
8149{
8150	if (wal_level != ControlFile->wal_level ||
8151		wal_log_hints != ControlFile->wal_log_hints ||
8152		MaxConnections != ControlFile->MaxConnections ||
8153		max_worker_processes != ControlFile->max_worker_processes ||
8154		max_wal_senders != ControlFile->max_wal_senders ||
8155		max_prepared_xacts != ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts ||
8156		max_locks_per_xact != ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact ||
8157		track_commit_timestamp != ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp)
8158	{
8159		/*
8160		 * The change in number of backend slots doesn't need to be WAL-logged
8161		 * if archiving is not enabled, as you can't start archive recovery
8162		 * with wal_level=minimal anyway. We don't really care about the
8163		 * values in pg_control either if wal_level=minimal, but seems better
8164		 * to keep them up-to-date to avoid confusion.
8165		 */
8166		if (wal_level != ControlFile->wal_level || XLogIsNeeded())
8167		{
8168			xl_parameter_change xlrec;
8169			XLogRecPtr	recptr;
8170
8171			xlrec.MaxConnections = MaxConnections;
8172			xlrec.max_worker_processes = max_worker_processes;
8173			xlrec.max_wal_senders = max_wal_senders;
8174			xlrec.max_prepared_xacts = max_prepared_xacts;
8175			xlrec.max_locks_per_xact = max_locks_per_xact;
8176			xlrec.wal_level = wal_level;
8177			xlrec.wal_log_hints = wal_log_hints;
8178			xlrec.track_commit_timestamp = track_commit_timestamp;
8179
8180			XLogBeginInsert();
8181			XLogRegisterData(&xlrec, sizeof(xlrec));
8182
8183			recptr = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_PARAMETER_CHANGE);
8184			XLogFlush(recptr);
8185		}
8186
8187		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8188
8189		ControlFile->MaxConnections = MaxConnections;
8190		ControlFile->max_worker_processes = max_worker_processes;
8191		ControlFile->max_wal_senders = max_wal_senders;
8192		ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts = max_prepared_xacts;
8193		ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact = max_locks_per_xact;
8194		ControlFile->wal_level = wal_level;
8195		ControlFile->wal_log_hints = wal_log_hints;
8196		ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp = track_commit_timestamp;
8197		UpdateControlFile();
8198
8199		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
8200	}
8201}
8202
8203/*
8204 * Update full_page_writes in shared memory, and write an
8205 * XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record if necessary.
8206 *
8207 * Note: this function assumes there is no other process running
8208 * concurrently that could update it.
8209 */
8210void
8211UpdateFullPageWrites(void)
8212{
8213	XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
8214	bool		recoveryInProgress;
8215
8216	/*
8217	 * Do nothing if full_page_writes has not been changed.
8218	 *
8219	 * It's safe to check the shared full_page_writes without the lock,
8220	 * because we assume that there is no concurrently running process which
8221	 * can update it.
8222	 */
8223	if (fullPageWrites == Insert->fullPageWrites)
8224		return;
8225
8226	/*
8227	 * Perform this outside critical section so that the WAL insert
8228	 * initialization done by RecoveryInProgress() doesn't trigger an
8229	 * assertion failure.
8230	 */
8231	recoveryInProgress = RecoveryInProgress();
8232
8233	START_CRIT_SECTION();
8234
8235	/*
8236	 * It's always safe to take full page images, even when not strictly
8237	 * required, but not the other round. So if we're setting full_page_writes
8238	 * to true, first set it true and then write the WAL record. If we're
8239	 * setting it to false, first write the WAL record and then set the global
8240	 * flag.
8241	 */
8242	if (fullPageWrites)
8243	{
8244		WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
8245		Insert->fullPageWrites = true;
8246		WALInsertLockRelease();
8247	}
8248
8249	/*
8250	 * Write an XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record. This allows us to keep track of
8251	 * full_page_writes during archive recovery, if required.
8252	 */
8253	if (XLogStandbyInfoActive() && !recoveryInProgress)
8254	{
8255		XLogBeginInsert();
8256		XLogRegisterData(&fullPageWrites, sizeof(bool));
8257
8258		XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_FPW_CHANGE);
8259	}
8260
8261	if (!fullPageWrites)
8262	{
8263		WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
8264		Insert->fullPageWrites = false;
8265		WALInsertLockRelease();
8266	}
8267	END_CRIT_SECTION();
8268}
8269
8270/*
8271 * XLOG resource manager's routines
8272 *
8273 * Definitions of info values are in include/catalog/pg_control.h, though
8274 * not all record types are related to control file updates.
8275 *
8276 * NOTE: Some XLOG record types that are directly related to WAL recovery
8277 * are handled in xlogrecovery_redo().
8278 */
8279void
8280xlog_redo(XLogReaderState *record)
8281{
8282	uint8		info = XLogRecGetInfo(record) & ~XLR_INFO_MASK;
8283	XLogRecPtr	lsn = record->EndRecPtr;
8284
8285	/*
8286	 * In XLOG rmgr, backup blocks are only used by XLOG_FPI and
8287	 * XLOG_FPI_FOR_HINT records.
8288	 */
8289	Assert(info == XLOG_FPI || info == XLOG_FPI_FOR_HINT ||
8290		   !XLogRecHasAnyBlockRefs(record));
8291
8292	if (info == XLOG_NEXTOID)
8293	{
8294		Oid			nextOid;
8295
8296		/*
8297		 * We used to try to take the maximum of TransamVariables->nextOid and
8298		 * the recorded nextOid, but that fails if the OID counter wraps
8299		 * around.  Since no OID allocation should be happening during replay
8300		 * anyway, better to just believe the record exactly.  We still take
8301		 * OidGenLock while setting the variable, just in case.
8302		 */
8303		memcpy(&nextOid, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(Oid));
8304		LWLockAcquire(OidGenLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8305		TransamVariables->nextOid = nextOid;
8306		TransamVariables->oidCount = 0;
8307		LWLockRelease(OidGenLock);
8308	}
8309	else if (info == XLOG_CHECKPOINT_SHUTDOWN)
8310	{
8311		CheckPoint	checkPoint;
8312		TimeLineID	replayTLI;
8313
8314		memcpy(&checkPoint, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(CheckPoint));
8315		/* In a SHUTDOWN checkpoint, believe the counters exactly */
8316		LWLockAcquire(XidGenLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8317		TransamVariables->nextXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8318		LWLockRelease(XidGenLock);
8319		LWLockAcquire(OidGenLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8320		TransamVariables->nextOid = checkPoint.nextOid;
8321		TransamVariables->oidCount = 0;
8322		LWLockRelease(OidGenLock);
8323		MultiXactSetNextMXact(checkPoint.nextMulti,
8324							  checkPoint.nextMultiOffset);
8325
8326		MultiXactAdvanceOldest(checkPoint.oldestMulti,
8327							   checkPoint.oldestMultiDB);
8328
8329		/*
8330		 * No need to set oldestClogXid here as well; it'll be set when we
8331		 * redo an xl_clog_truncate if it changed since initialization.
8332		 */
8333		SetTransactionIdLimit(checkPoint.oldestXid, checkPoint.oldestXidDB);
8334
8335		/*
8336		 * If we see a shutdown checkpoint while waiting for an end-of-backup
8337		 * record, the backup was canceled and the end-of-backup record will
8338		 * never arrive.
8339		 */
8340		if (ArchiveRecoveryRequested &&
8341			!XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(ControlFile->backupStartPoint) &&
8342			XLogRecPtrIsInvalid(ControlFile->backupEndPoint))
8343			ereport(PANIC,
8344					(errmsg("online backup was canceled, recovery cannot continue")));
8345
8346		/*
8347		 * If we see a shutdown checkpoint, we know that nothing was running
8348		 * on the primary at this point. So fake-up an empty running-xacts
8349		 * record and use that here and now. Recover additional standby state
8350		 * for prepared transactions.
8351		 */
8352		if (standbyState >= STANDBY_INITIALIZED)
8353		{
8354			TransactionId *xids;
8355			int			nxids;
8356			TransactionId oldestActiveXID;
8357			TransactionId latestCompletedXid;
8358			RunningTransactionsData running;
8359
8360			oldestActiveXID = PrescanPreparedTransactions(&xids, &nxids);
8361
8362			/* Update pg_subtrans entries for any prepared transactions */
8363			StandbyRecoverPreparedTransactions();
8364
8365			/*
8366			 * Construct a RunningTransactions snapshot representing a shut
8367			 * down server, with only prepared transactions still alive. We're
8368			 * never overflowed at this point because all subxids are listed
8369			 * with their parent prepared transactions.
8370			 */
8371			running.xcnt = nxids;
8372			running.subxcnt = 0;
8373			running.subxid_status = SUBXIDS_IN_SUBTRANS;
8374			running.nextXid = XidFromFullTransactionId(checkPoint.nextXid);
8375			running.oldestRunningXid = oldestActiveXID;
8376			latestCompletedXid = XidFromFullTransactionId(checkPoint.nextXid);
8377			TransactionIdRetreat(latestCompletedXid);
8378			Assert(TransactionIdIsNormal(latestCompletedXid));
8379			running.latestCompletedXid = latestCompletedXid;
8380			running.xids = xids;
8381
8382			ProcArrayApplyRecoveryInfo(&running);
8383		}
8384
8385		/* ControlFile->checkPointCopy always tracks the latest ckpt XID */
8386		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8387		ControlFile->checkPointCopy.nextXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8388		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
8389
8390		/* Update shared-memory copy of checkpoint XID/epoch */
8391		SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8392		XLogCtl->ckptFullXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8393		SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8394
8395		/*
8396		 * We should've already switched to the new TLI before replaying this
8397		 * record.
8398		 */
8399		(void) GetCurrentReplayRecPtr(&replayTLI);
8400		if (checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID != replayTLI)
8401			ereport(PANIC,
8402					(errmsg("unexpected timeline ID %u (should be %u) in shutdown checkpoint record",
8403							checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID, replayTLI)));
8404
8405		RecoveryRestartPoint(&checkPoint, record);
8406
8407		/*
8408		 * After replaying a checkpoint record, free all smgr objects.
8409		 * Otherwise we would never do so for dropped relations, as the
8410		 * startup does not process shared invalidation messages or call
8411		 * AtEOXact_SMgr().
8412		 */
8413		smgrdestroyall();
8414	}
8415	else if (info == XLOG_CHECKPOINT_ONLINE)
8416	{
8417		CheckPoint	checkPoint;
8418		TimeLineID	replayTLI;
8419
8420		memcpy(&checkPoint, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(CheckPoint));
8421		/* In an ONLINE checkpoint, treat the XID counter as a minimum */
8422		LWLockAcquire(XidGenLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8423		if (FullTransactionIdPrecedes(TransamVariables->nextXid,
8424									  checkPoint.nextXid))
8425			TransamVariables->nextXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8426		LWLockRelease(XidGenLock);
8427
8428		/*
8429		 * We ignore the nextOid counter in an ONLINE checkpoint, preferring
8430		 * to track OID assignment through XLOG_NEXTOID records.  The nextOid
8431		 * counter is from the start of the checkpoint and might well be stale
8432		 * compared to later XLOG_NEXTOID records.  We could try to take the
8433		 * maximum of the nextOid counter and our latest value, but since
8434		 * there's no particular guarantee about the speed with which the OID
8435		 * counter wraps around, that's a risky thing to do.  In any case,
8436		 * users of the nextOid counter are required to avoid assignment of
8437		 * duplicates, so that a somewhat out-of-date value should be safe.
8438		 */
8439
8440		/* Handle multixact */
8441		MultiXactAdvanceNextMXact(checkPoint.nextMulti,
8442								  checkPoint.nextMultiOffset);
8443
8444		/*
8445		 * NB: This may perform multixact truncation when replaying WAL
8446		 * generated by an older primary.
8447		 */
8448		MultiXactAdvanceOldest(checkPoint.oldestMulti,
8449							   checkPoint.oldestMultiDB);
8450		if (TransactionIdPrecedes(TransamVariables->oldestXid,
8451								  checkPoint.oldestXid))
8452			SetTransactionIdLimit(checkPoint.oldestXid,
8453								  checkPoint.oldestXidDB);
8454		/* ControlFile->checkPointCopy always tracks the latest ckpt XID */
8455		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8456		ControlFile->checkPointCopy.nextXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8457		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
8458
8459		/* Update shared-memory copy of checkpoint XID/epoch */
8460		SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8461		XLogCtl->ckptFullXid = checkPoint.nextXid;
8462		SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8463
8464		/* TLI should not change in an on-line checkpoint */
8465		(void) GetCurrentReplayRecPtr(&replayTLI);
8466		if (checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID != replayTLI)
8467			ereport(PANIC,
8468					(errmsg("unexpected timeline ID %u (should be %u) in online checkpoint record",
8469							checkPoint.ThisTimeLineID, replayTLI)));
8470
8471		RecoveryRestartPoint(&checkPoint, record);
8472
8473		/*
8474		 * After replaying a checkpoint record, free all smgr objects.
8475		 * Otherwise we would never do so for dropped relations, as the
8476		 * startup does not process shared invalidation messages or call
8477		 * AtEOXact_SMgr().
8478		 */
8479		smgrdestroyall();
8480	}
8481	else if (info == XLOG_OVERWRITE_CONTRECORD)
8482	{
8483		/* nothing to do here, handled in xlogrecovery_redo() */
8484	}
8485	else if (info == XLOG_END_OF_RECOVERY)
8486	{
8487		xl_end_of_recovery xlrec;
8488		TimeLineID	replayTLI;
8489
8490		memcpy(&xlrec, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(xl_end_of_recovery));
8491
8492		/*
8493		 * For Hot Standby, we could treat this like a Shutdown Checkpoint,
8494		 * but this case is rarer and harder to test, so the benefit doesn't
8495		 * outweigh the potential extra cost of maintenance.
8496		 */
8497
8498		/*
8499		 * We should've already switched to the new TLI before replaying this
8500		 * record.
8501		 */
8502		(void) GetCurrentReplayRecPtr(&replayTLI);
8503		if (xlrec.ThisTimeLineID != replayTLI)
8504			ereport(PANIC,
8505					(errmsg("unexpected timeline ID %u (should be %u) in end-of-recovery record",
8506							xlrec.ThisTimeLineID, replayTLI)));
8507	}
8508	else if (info == XLOG_NOOP)
8509	{
8510		/* nothing to do here */
8511	}
8512	else if (info == XLOG_SWITCH)
8513	{
8514		/* nothing to do here */
8515	}
8516	else if (info == XLOG_RESTORE_POINT)
8517	{
8518		/* nothing to do here, handled in xlogrecovery.c */
8519	}
8520	else if (info == XLOG_FPI || info == XLOG_FPI_FOR_HINT)
8521	{
8522		/*
8523		 * XLOG_FPI records contain nothing else but one or more block
8524		 * references. Every block reference must include a full-page image
8525		 * even if full_page_writes was disabled when the record was generated
8526		 * - otherwise there would be no point in this record.
8527		 *
8528		 * XLOG_FPI_FOR_HINT records are generated when a page needs to be
8529		 * WAL-logged because of a hint bit update. They are only generated
8530		 * when checksums and/or wal_log_hints are enabled. They may include
8531		 * no full-page images if full_page_writes was disabled when they were
8532		 * generated. In this case there is nothing to do here.
8533		 *
8534		 * No recovery conflicts are generated by these generic records - if a
8535		 * resource manager needs to generate conflicts, it has to define a
8536		 * separate WAL record type and redo routine.
8537		 */
8538		for (uint8 block_id = 0; block_id <= XLogRecMaxBlockId(record); block_id++)
8539		{
8540			Buffer		buffer;
8541
8542			if (!XLogRecHasBlockImage(record, block_id))
8543			{
8544				if (info == XLOG_FPI)
8545					elog(ERROR, "XLOG_FPI record did not contain a full-page image");
8546				continue;
8547			}
8548
8549			if (XLogReadBufferForRedo(record, block_id, &buffer) != BLK_RESTORED)
8550				elog(ERROR, "unexpected XLogReadBufferForRedo result when restoring backup block");
8551			UnlockReleaseBuffer(buffer);
8552		}
8553	}
8554	else if (info == XLOG_BACKUP_END)
8555	{
8556		/* nothing to do here, handled in xlogrecovery_redo() */
8557	}
8558	else if (info == XLOG_PARAMETER_CHANGE)
8559	{
8560		xl_parameter_change xlrec;
8561
8562		/* Update our copy of the parameters in pg_control */
8563		memcpy(&xlrec, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(xl_parameter_change));
8564
8565		/*
8566		 * Invalidate logical slots if we are in hot standby and the primary
8567		 * does not have a WAL level sufficient for logical decoding. No need
8568		 * to search for potentially conflicting logically slots if standby is
8569		 * running with wal_level lower than logical, because in that case, we
8570		 * would have either disallowed creation of logical slots or
8571		 * invalidated existing ones.
8572		 */
8573		if (InRecovery && InHotStandby &&
8574			xlrec.wal_level < WAL_LEVEL_LOGICAL &&
8575			wal_level >= WAL_LEVEL_LOGICAL)
8576			InvalidateObsoleteReplicationSlots(RS_INVAL_WAL_LEVEL,
8577											   0, InvalidOid,
8578											   InvalidTransactionId);
8579
8580		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
8581		ControlFile->MaxConnections = xlrec.MaxConnections;
8582		ControlFile->max_worker_processes = xlrec.max_worker_processes;
8583		ControlFile->max_wal_senders = xlrec.max_wal_senders;
8584		ControlFile->max_prepared_xacts = xlrec.max_prepared_xacts;
8585		ControlFile->max_locks_per_xact = xlrec.max_locks_per_xact;
8586		ControlFile->wal_level = xlrec.wal_level;
8587		ControlFile->wal_log_hints = xlrec.wal_log_hints;
8588
8589		/*
8590		 * Update minRecoveryPoint to ensure that if recovery is aborted, we
8591		 * recover back up to this point before allowing hot standby again.
8592		 * This is important if the max_* settings are decreased, to ensure
8593		 * you don't run queries against the WAL preceding the change. The
8594		 * local copies cannot be updated as long as crash recovery is
8595		 * happening and we expect all the WAL to be replayed.
8596		 */
8597		if (InArchiveRecovery)
8598		{
8599			LocalMinRecoveryPoint = ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint;
8600			LocalMinRecoveryPointTLI = ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI;
8601		}
8602		if (LocalMinRecoveryPoint != InvalidXLogRecPtr && LocalMinRecoveryPoint < lsn)
8603		{
8604			TimeLineID	replayTLI;
8605
8606			(void) GetCurrentReplayRecPtr(&replayTLI);
8607			ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint = lsn;
8608			ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI = replayTLI;
8609		}
8610
8611		CommitTsParameterChange(xlrec.track_commit_timestamp,
8612								ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp);
8613		ControlFile->track_commit_timestamp = xlrec.track_commit_timestamp;
8614
8615		UpdateControlFile();
8616		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
8617
8618		/* Check to see if any parameter change gives a problem on recovery */
8619		CheckRequiredParameterValues();
8620	}
8621	else if (info == XLOG_FPW_CHANGE)
8622	{
8623		bool		fpw;
8624
8625		memcpy(&fpw, XLogRecGetData(record), sizeof(bool));
8626
8627		/*
8628		 * Update the LSN of the last replayed XLOG_FPW_CHANGE record so that
8629		 * do_pg_backup_start() and do_pg_backup_stop() can check whether
8630		 * full_page_writes has been disabled during online backup.
8631		 */
8632		if (!fpw)
8633		{
8634			SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8635			if (XLogCtl->lastFpwDisableRecPtr < record->ReadRecPtr)
8636				XLogCtl->lastFpwDisableRecPtr = record->ReadRecPtr;
8637			SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8638		}
8639
8640		/* Keep track of full_page_writes */
8641		lastFullPageWrites = fpw;
8642	}
8643	else if (info == XLOG_CHECKPOINT_REDO)
8644	{
8645		/* nothing to do here, just for informational purposes */
8646	}
8647}
8648
8649/*
8650 * Return the extra open flags used for opening a file, depending on the
8651 * value of the GUCs wal_sync_method, fsync and debug_io_direct.
8652 */
8653static int
8654get_sync_bit(int method)
8655{
8656	int			o_direct_flag = 0;
8657
8658	/*
8659	 * Use O_DIRECT if requested, except in walreceiver process.  The WAL
8660	 * written by walreceiver is normally read by the startup process soon
8661	 * after it's written.  Also, walreceiver performs unaligned writes, which
8662	 * don't work with O_DIRECT, so it is required for correctness too.
8663	 */
8664	if ((io_direct_flags & IO_DIRECT_WAL) && !AmWalReceiverProcess())
8665		o_direct_flag = PG_O_DIRECT;
8666
8667	/* If fsync is disabled, never open in sync mode */
8668	if (!enableFsync)
8669		return o_direct_flag;
8670
8671	switch (method)
8672	{
8673			/*
8674			 * enum values for all sync options are defined even if they are
8675			 * not supported on the current platform.  But if not, they are
8676			 * not included in the enum option array, and therefore will never
8677			 * be seen here.
8678			 */
8679		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC:
8680		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC_WRITETHROUGH:
8681		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FDATASYNC:
8682			return o_direct_flag;
8683#ifdef O_SYNC
8684		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN:
8685			return O_SYNC | o_direct_flag;
8686#endif
8687#ifdef O_DSYNC
8688		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN_DSYNC:
8689			return O_DSYNC | o_direct_flag;
8690#endif
8691		default:
8692			/* can't happen (unless we are out of sync with option array) */
8693			elog(ERROR, "unrecognized \"wal_sync_method\": %d", method);
8694			return 0;			/* silence warning */
8695	}
8696}
8697
8698/*
8699 * GUC support
8700 */
8701void
8702assign_wal_sync_method(int new_wal_sync_method, void *extra)
8703{
8704	if (wal_sync_method != new_wal_sync_method)
8705	{
8706		/*
8707		 * To ensure that no blocks escape unsynced, force an fsync on the
8708		 * currently open log segment (if any).  Also, if the open flag is
8709		 * changing, close the log file so it will be reopened (with new flag
8710		 * bit) at next use.
8711		 */
8712		if (openLogFile >= 0)
8713		{
8714			pgstat_report_wait_start(WAIT_EVENT_WAL_SYNC_METHOD_ASSIGN);
8715			if (pg_fsync(openLogFile) != 0)
8716			{
8717				char		xlogfname[MAXFNAMELEN];
8718				int			save_errno;
8719
8720				save_errno = errno;
8721				XLogFileName(xlogfname, openLogTLI, openLogSegNo,
8722							 wal_segment_size);
8723				errno = save_errno;
8724				ereport(PANIC,
8725						(errcode_for_file_access(),
8726						 errmsg("could not fsync file \"%s\": %m", xlogfname)));
8727			}
8728
8729			pgstat_report_wait_end();
8730			if (get_sync_bit(wal_sync_method) != get_sync_bit(new_wal_sync_method))
8731				XLogFileClose();
8732		}
8733	}
8734}
8735
8736
8737/*
8738 * Issue appropriate kind of fsync (if any) for an XLOG output file.
8739 *
8740 * 'fd' is a file descriptor for the XLOG file to be fsync'd.
8741 * 'segno' is for error reporting purposes.
8742 */
8743void
8744issue_xlog_fsync(int fd, XLogSegNo segno, TimeLineID tli)
8745{
8746	char	   *msg = NULL;
8747	instr_time	start;
8748
8749	Assert(tli != 0);
8750
8751	/*
8752	 * Quick exit if fsync is disabled or write() has already synced the WAL
8753	 * file.
8754	 */
8755	if (!enableFsync ||
8756		wal_sync_method == WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN ||
8757		wal_sync_method == WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN_DSYNC)
8758		return;
8759
8760	/*
8761	 * Measure I/O timing to sync the WAL file for pg_stat_io.
8762	 */
8763	start = pgstat_prepare_io_time(track_wal_io_timing);
8764
8765	pgstat_report_wait_start(WAIT_EVENT_WAL_SYNC);
8766	switch (wal_sync_method)
8767	{
8768		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC:
8769			if (pg_fsync_no_writethrough(fd) != 0)
8770				msg = _("could not fsync file \"%s\": %m");
8771			break;
8772#ifdef HAVE_FSYNC_WRITETHROUGH
8773		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FSYNC_WRITETHROUGH:
8774			if (pg_fsync_writethrough(fd) != 0)
8775				msg = _("could not fsync write-through file \"%s\": %m");
8776			break;
8777#endif
8778		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_FDATASYNC:
8779			if (pg_fdatasync(fd) != 0)
8780				msg = _("could not fdatasync file \"%s\": %m");
8781			break;
8782		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN:
8783		case WAL_SYNC_METHOD_OPEN_DSYNC:
8784			/* not reachable */
8785			Assert(false);
8786			break;
8787		default:
8788			ereport(PANIC,
8789					errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAMETER_VALUE),
8790					errmsg_internal("unrecognized \"wal_sync_method\": %d", wal_sync_method));
8791			break;
8792	}
8793
8794	/* PANIC if failed to fsync */
8795	if (msg)
8796	{
8797		char		xlogfname[MAXFNAMELEN];
8798		int			save_errno = errno;
8799
8800		XLogFileName(xlogfname, tli, segno, wal_segment_size);
8801		errno = save_errno;
8802		ereport(PANIC,
8803				(errcode_for_file_access(),
8804				 errmsg(msg, xlogfname)));
8805	}
8806
8807	pgstat_report_wait_end();
8808
8809	pgstat_count_io_op_time(IOOBJECT_WAL, IOCONTEXT_NORMAL, IOOP_FSYNC,
8810							start, 1, 0);
8811}
8812
8813/*
8814 * do_pg_backup_start is the workhorse of the user-visible pg_backup_start()
8815 * function. It creates the necessary starting checkpoint and constructs the
8816 * backup state and tablespace map.
8817 *
8818 * Input parameters are "state" (the backup state), "fast" (if true, we do
8819 * the checkpoint in immediate mode to make it faster), and "tablespaces"
8820 * (if non-NULL, indicates a list of tablespaceinfo structs describing the
8821 * cluster's tablespaces.).
8822 *
