8823 * The tablespace map contents are appended to passed-in parameter
8824 * tablespace_map and the caller is responsible for including it in the backup
8825 * archive as 'tablespace_map'. The tablespace_map file is required mainly for
8826 * tar format in windows as native windows utilities are not able to create
8827 * symlinks while extracting files from tar. However for consistency and
8828 * platform-independence, we do it the same way everywhere.
8829 *
8830 * It fills in "state" with the information required for the backup, such
8831 * as the minimum WAL location that must be present to restore from this
8832 * backup (starttli) and the corresponding timeline ID (starttli).
8833 *
8834 * Every successfully started backup must be stopped by calling
8835 * do_pg_backup_stop() or do_pg_abort_backup(). There can be many
8836 * backups active at the same time.
8837 *
8838 * It is the responsibility of the caller of this function to verify the
8839 * permissions of the calling user!
8840 */
8841void
8842do_pg_backup_start(const char *backupidstr, bool fast, List **tablespaces,
8843				   BackupState *state, StringInfo tblspcmapfile)
8844{
8845	bool		backup_started_in_recovery;
8846
8847	Assert(state != NULL);
8848	backup_started_in_recovery = RecoveryInProgress();
8849
8850	/*
8851	 * During recovery, we don't need to check WAL level. Because, if WAL
8852	 * level is not sufficient, it's impossible to get here during recovery.
8853	 */
8854	if (!backup_started_in_recovery && !XLogIsNeeded())
8855		ereport(ERROR,
8856				(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
8857				 errmsg("WAL level not sufficient for making an online backup"),
8858				 errhint("\"wal_level\" must be set to \"replica\" or \"logical\" at server start.")));
8859
8860	if (strlen(backupidstr) > MAXPGPATH)
8861		ereport(ERROR,
8862				(errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_PARAMETER_VALUE),
8863				 errmsg("backup label too long (max %d bytes)",
8864						MAXPGPATH)));
8865
8866	strlcpy(state->name, backupidstr, sizeof(state->name));
8867
8868	/*
8869	 * Mark backup active in shared memory.  We must do full-page WAL writes
8870	 * during an on-line backup even if not doing so at other times, because
8871	 * it's quite possible for the backup dump to obtain a "torn" (partially
8872	 * written) copy of a database page if it reads the page concurrently with
8873	 * our write to the same page.  This can be fixed as long as the first
8874	 * write to the page in the WAL sequence is a full-page write. Hence, we
8875	 * increment runningBackups then force a CHECKPOINT, to ensure there are
8876	 * no dirty pages in shared memory that might get dumped while the backup
8877	 * is in progress without having a corresponding WAL record.  (Once the
8878	 * backup is complete, we need not force full-page writes anymore, since
8879	 * we expect that any pages not modified during the backup interval must
8880	 * have been correctly captured by the backup.)
8881	 *
8882	 * Note that forcing full-page writes has no effect during an online
8883	 * backup from the standby.
8884	 *
8885	 * We must hold all the insertion locks to change the value of
8886	 * runningBackups, to ensure adequate interlocking against
8887	 * XLogInsertRecord().
8888	 */
8889	WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
8890	XLogCtl->Insert.runningBackups++;
8891	WALInsertLockRelease();
8892
8893	/*
8894	 * Ensure we decrement runningBackups if we fail below. NB -- for this to
8895	 * work correctly, it is critical that sessionBackupState is only updated
8896	 * after this block is over.
8897	 */
8898	PG_ENSURE_ERROR_CLEANUP(do_pg_abort_backup, DatumGetBool(true));
8899	{
8900		bool		gotUniqueStartpoint = false;
8901		DIR		   *tblspcdir;
8902		struct dirent *de;
8903		tablespaceinfo *ti;
8904		int			datadirpathlen;
8905
8906		/*
8907		 * Force an XLOG file switch before the checkpoint, to ensure that the
8908		 * WAL segment the checkpoint is written to doesn't contain pages with
8909		 * old timeline IDs.  That would otherwise happen if you called
8910		 * pg_backup_start() right after restoring from a PITR archive: the
8911		 * first WAL segment containing the startup checkpoint has pages in
8912		 * the beginning with the old timeline ID.  That can cause trouble at
8913		 * recovery: we won't have a history file covering the old timeline if
8914		 * pg_wal directory was not included in the base backup and the WAL
8915		 * archive was cleared too before starting the backup.
8916		 *
8917		 * This also ensures that we have emitted a WAL page header that has
8918		 * XLP_BKP_REMOVABLE off before we emit the checkpoint record.
8919		 * Therefore, if a WAL archiver (such as pglesslog) is trying to
8920		 * compress out removable backup blocks, it won't remove any that
8921		 * occur after this point.
8922		 *
8923		 * During recovery, we skip forcing XLOG file switch, which means that
8924		 * the backup taken during recovery is not available for the special
8925		 * recovery case described above.
8926		 */
8927		if (!backup_started_in_recovery)
8928			RequestXLogSwitch(false);
8929
8930		do
8931		{
8932			bool		checkpointfpw;
8933
8934			/*
8935			 * Force a CHECKPOINT.  Aside from being necessary to prevent torn
8936			 * page problems, this guarantees that two successive backup runs
8937			 * will have different checkpoint positions and hence different
8938			 * history file names, even if nothing happened in between.
8939			 *
8940			 * During recovery, establish a restartpoint if possible. We use
8941			 * the last restartpoint as the backup starting checkpoint. This
8942			 * means that two successive backup runs can have same checkpoint
8943			 * positions.
8944			 *
8945			 * Since the fact that we are executing do_pg_backup_start()
8946			 * during recovery means that checkpointer is running, we can use
8947			 * RequestCheckpoint() to establish a restartpoint.
8948			 *
8949			 * We use CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE only if requested by user (via
8950			 * passing fast = true).  Otherwise this can take awhile.
8951			 */
8952			RequestCheckpoint(CHECKPOINT_FORCE | CHECKPOINT_WAIT |
8953							  (fast ? CHECKPOINT_IMMEDIATE : 0));
8954
8955			/*
8956			 * Now we need to fetch the checkpoint record location, and also
8957			 * its REDO pointer.  The oldest point in WAL that would be needed
8958			 * to restore starting from the checkpoint is precisely the REDO
8959			 * pointer.
8960			 */
8961			LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_SHARED);
8962			state->checkpointloc = ControlFile->checkPoint;
8963			state->startpoint = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo;
8964			state->starttli = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.ThisTimeLineID;
8965			checkpointfpw = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.fullPageWrites;
8966			LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
8967
8968			if (backup_started_in_recovery)
8969			{
8970				XLogRecPtr	recptr;
8971
8972				/*
8973				 * Check to see if all WAL replayed during online backup
8974				 * (i.e., since last restartpoint used as backup starting
8975				 * checkpoint) contain full-page writes.
8976				 */
8977				SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8978				recptr = XLogCtl->lastFpwDisableRecPtr;
8979				SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
8980
8981				if (!checkpointfpw || state->startpoint <= recptr)
8982					ereport(ERROR,
8983							(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
8984							 errmsg("WAL generated with \"full_page_writes=off\" was replayed "
8985									"since last restartpoint"),
8986							 errhint("This means that the backup being taken on the standby "
8987									 "is corrupt and should not be used. "
8988									 "Enable \"full_page_writes\" and run CHECKPOINT on the primary, "
8989									 "and then try an online backup again.")));
8990
8991				/*
8992				 * During recovery, since we don't use the end-of-backup WAL
8993				 * record and don't write the backup history file, the
8994				 * starting WAL location doesn't need to be unique. This means
8995				 * that two base backups started at the same time might use
8996				 * the same checkpoint as starting locations.
8997				 */
8998				gotUniqueStartpoint = true;
8999			}
9000
9001			/*
9002			 * If two base backups are started at the same time (in WAL sender
9003			 * processes), we need to make sure that they use different
9004			 * checkpoints as starting locations, because we use the starting
9005			 * WAL location as a unique identifier for the base backup in the
9006			 * end-of-backup WAL record and when we write the backup history
9007			 * file. Perhaps it would be better generate a separate unique ID
9008			 * for each backup instead of forcing another checkpoint, but
9009			 * taking a checkpoint right after another is not that expensive
9010			 * either because only few buffers have been dirtied yet.
9011			 */
9012			WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
9013			if (XLogCtl->Insert.lastBackupStart < state->startpoint)
9014			{
9015				XLogCtl->Insert.lastBackupStart = state->startpoint;
9016				gotUniqueStartpoint = true;
9017			}
9018			WALInsertLockRelease();
9019		} while (!gotUniqueStartpoint);
9020
9021		/*
9022		 * Construct tablespace_map file.
9023		 */
9024		datadirpathlen = strlen(DataDir);
9025
9026		/* Collect information about all tablespaces */
9027		tblspcdir = AllocateDir(PG_TBLSPC_DIR);
9028		while ((de = ReadDir(tblspcdir, PG_TBLSPC_DIR)) != NULL)
9029		{
9030			char		fullpath[MAXPGPATH + sizeof(PG_TBLSPC_DIR)];
9031			char		linkpath[MAXPGPATH];
9032			char	   *relpath = NULL;
9033			char	   *s;
9034			PGFileType	de_type;
9035			char	   *badp;
9036			Oid			tsoid;
9037
9038			/*
9039			 * Try to parse the directory name as an unsigned integer.
9040			 *
9041			 * Tablespace directories should be positive integers that can be
9042			 * represented in 32 bits, with no leading zeroes or trailing
9043			 * garbage. If we come across a name that doesn't meet those
9044			 * criteria, skip it.
9045			 */
9046			if (de->d_name[0] < '1' || de->d_name[1] > '9')
9047				continue;
9048			errno = 0;
9049			tsoid = strtoul(de->d_name, &badp, 10);
9050			if (*badp != '\0' || errno == EINVAL || errno == ERANGE)
9051				continue;
9052
9053			snprintf(fullpath, sizeof(fullpath), "%s/%s", PG_TBLSPC_DIR, de->d_name);
9054
9055			de_type = get_dirent_type(fullpath, de, false, ERROR);
9056
9057			if (de_type == PGFILETYPE_LNK)
9058			{
9059				StringInfoData escapedpath;
9060				int			rllen;
9061
9062				rllen = readlink(fullpath, linkpath, sizeof(linkpath));
9063				if (rllen < 0)
9064				{
9065					ereport(WARNING,
9066							(errmsg("could not read symbolic link \"%s\": %m",
9067									fullpath)));
9068					continue;
9069				}
9070				else if (rllen >= sizeof(linkpath))
9071				{
9072					ereport(WARNING,
9073							(errmsg("symbolic link \"%s\" target is too long",
9074									fullpath)));
9075					continue;
9076				}
9077				linkpath[rllen] = '\0';
9078
9079				/*
9080				 * Relpath holds the relative path of the tablespace directory
9081				 * when it's located within PGDATA, or NULL if it's located
9082				 * elsewhere.
9083				 */
9084				if (rllen > datadirpathlen &&
9085					strncmp(linkpath, DataDir, datadirpathlen) == 0 &&
9086					IS_DIR_SEP(linkpath[datadirpathlen]))
9087					relpath = pstrdup(linkpath + datadirpathlen + 1);
9088
9089				/*
9090				 * Add a backslash-escaped version of the link path to the
9091				 * tablespace map file.
9092				 */
9093				initStringInfo(&escapedpath);
9094				for (s = linkpath; *s; s++)
9095				{
9096					if (*s == '\n' || *s == '\r' || *s == '\\')
9097						appendStringInfoChar(&escapedpath, '\\');
9098					appendStringInfoChar(&escapedpath, *s);
9099				}
9100				appendStringInfo(tblspcmapfile, "%s %s\n",
9101								 de->d_name, escapedpath.data);
9102				pfree(escapedpath.data);
9103			}
9104			else if (de_type == PGFILETYPE_DIR)
9105			{
9106				/*
9107				 * It's possible to use allow_in_place_tablespaces to create
9108				 * directories directly under pg_tblspc, for testing purposes
9109				 * only.
9110				 *
9111				 * In this case, we store a relative path rather than an
9112				 * absolute path into the tablespaceinfo.
9113				 */
9114				snprintf(linkpath, sizeof(linkpath), "%s/%s",
9115						 PG_TBLSPC_DIR, de->d_name);
9116				relpath = pstrdup(linkpath);
9117			}
9118			else
9119			{
9120				/* Skip any other file type that appears here. */
9121				continue;
9122			}
9123
9124			ti = palloc(sizeof(tablespaceinfo));
9125			ti->oid = tsoid;
9126			ti->path = pstrdup(linkpath);
9127			ti->rpath = relpath;
9128			ti->size = -1;
9129
9130			if (tablespaces)
9131				*tablespaces = lappend(*tablespaces, ti);
9132		}
9133		FreeDir(tblspcdir);
9134
9135		state->starttime = (pg_time_t) time(NULL);
9136	}
9137	PG_END_ENSURE_ERROR_CLEANUP(do_pg_abort_backup, DatumGetBool(true));
9138
9139	state->started_in_recovery = backup_started_in_recovery;
9140
9141	/*
9142	 * Mark that the start phase has correctly finished for the backup.
9143	 */
9144	sessionBackupState = SESSION_BACKUP_RUNNING;
9145}
9146
9147/*
9148 * Utility routine to fetch the session-level status of a backup running.
9149 */
9150SessionBackupState
9151get_backup_status(void)
9152{
9153	return sessionBackupState;
9154}
9155
9156/*
9157 * do_pg_backup_stop
9158 *
9159 * Utility function called at the end of an online backup.  It creates history
9160 * file (if required), resets sessionBackupState and so on.  It can optionally
9161 * wait for WAL segments to be archived.
9162 *
9163 * "state" is filled with the information necessary to restore from this
9164 * backup with its stop LSN (stoppoint), its timeline ID (stoptli), etc.
9165 *
9166 * It is the responsibility of the caller of this function to verify the
9167 * permissions of the calling user!
9168 */
9169void
9170do_pg_backup_stop(BackupState *state, bool waitforarchive)
9171{
9172	bool		backup_stopped_in_recovery = false;
9173	char		histfilepath[MAXPGPATH];
9174	char		lastxlogfilename[MAXFNAMELEN];
9175	char		histfilename[MAXFNAMELEN];
9176	XLogSegNo	_logSegNo;
9177	FILE	   *fp;
9178	int			seconds_before_warning;
9179	int			waits = 0;
9180	bool		reported_waiting = false;
9181
9182	Assert(state != NULL);
9183
9184	backup_stopped_in_recovery = RecoveryInProgress();
9185
9186	/*
9187	 * During recovery, we don't need to check WAL level. Because, if WAL
9188	 * level is not sufficient, it's impossible to get here during recovery.
9189	 */
9190	if (!backup_stopped_in_recovery && !XLogIsNeeded())
9191		ereport(ERROR,
9192				(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
9193				 errmsg("WAL level not sufficient for making an online backup"),
9194				 errhint("\"wal_level\" must be set to \"replica\" or \"logical\" at server start.")));
9195
9196	/*
9197	 * OK to update backup counter and session-level lock.
9198	 *
9199	 * Note that CHECK_FOR_INTERRUPTS() must not occur while updating them,
9200	 * otherwise they can be updated inconsistently, which might cause
9201	 * do_pg_abort_backup() to fail.
9202	 */
9203	WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
9204
9205	/*
9206	 * It is expected that each do_pg_backup_start() call is matched by
9207	 * exactly one do_pg_backup_stop() call.
9208	 */
9209	Assert(XLogCtl->Insert.runningBackups > 0);
9210	XLogCtl->Insert.runningBackups--;
9211
9212	/*
9213	 * Clean up session-level lock.
9214	 *
9215	 * You might think that WALInsertLockRelease() can be called before
9216	 * cleaning up session-level lock because session-level lock doesn't need
9217	 * to be protected with WAL insertion lock. But since
9218	 * CHECK_FOR_INTERRUPTS() can occur in it, session-level lock must be
9219	 * cleaned up before it.
9220	 */
9221	sessionBackupState = SESSION_BACKUP_NONE;
9222
9223	WALInsertLockRelease();
9224
9225	/*
9226	 * If we are taking an online backup from the standby, we confirm that the
9227	 * standby has not been promoted during the backup.
9228	 */
9229	if (state->started_in_recovery && !backup_stopped_in_recovery)
9230		ereport(ERROR,
9231				(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
9232				 errmsg("the standby was promoted during online backup"),
9233				 errhint("This means that the backup being taken is corrupt "
9234						 "and should not be used. "
9235						 "Try taking another online backup.")));
9236
9237	/*
9238	 * During recovery, we don't write an end-of-backup record. We assume that
9239	 * pg_control was backed up last and its minimum recovery point can be
9240	 * available as the backup end location. Since we don't have an
9241	 * end-of-backup record, we use the pg_control value to check whether
9242	 * we've reached the end of backup when starting recovery from this
9243	 * backup. We have no way of checking if pg_control wasn't backed up last
9244	 * however.
9245	 *
9246	 * We don't force a switch to new WAL file but it is still possible to
9247	 * wait for all the required files to be archived if waitforarchive is
9248	 * true. This is okay if we use the backup to start a standby and fetch
9249	 * the missing WAL using streaming replication. But in the case of an
9250	 * archive recovery, a user should set waitforarchive to true and wait for
9251	 * them to be archived to ensure that all the required files are
9252	 * available.
9253	 *
9254	 * We return the current minimum recovery point as the backup end
9255	 * location. Note that it can be greater than the exact backup end
9256	 * location if the minimum recovery point is updated after the backup of
9257	 * pg_control. This is harmless for current uses.
9258	 *
9259	 * XXX currently a backup history file is for informational and debug
9260	 * purposes only. It's not essential for an online backup. Furthermore,
9261	 * even if it's created, it will not be archived during recovery because
9262	 * an archiver is not invoked. So it doesn't seem worthwhile to write a
9263	 * backup history file during recovery.
9264	 */
9265	if (backup_stopped_in_recovery)
9266	{
9267		XLogRecPtr	recptr;
9268
9269		/*
9270		 * Check to see if all WAL replayed during online backup contain
9271		 * full-page writes.
9272		 */
9273		SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
9274		recptr = XLogCtl->lastFpwDisableRecPtr;
9275		SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
9276
9277		if (state->startpoint <= recptr)
9278			ereport(ERROR,
9279					(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
9280					 errmsg("WAL generated with \"full_page_writes=off\" was replayed "
9281							"during online backup"),
9282					 errhint("This means that the backup being taken on the standby "
9283							 "is corrupt and should not be used. "
9284							 "Enable \"full_page_writes\" and run CHECKPOINT on the primary, "
9285							 "and then try an online backup again.")));
9286
9287
9288		LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_SHARED);
9289		state->stoppoint = ControlFile->minRecoveryPoint;
9290		state->stoptli = ControlFile->minRecoveryPointTLI;
9291		LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
9292	}
9293	else
9294	{
9295		char	   *history_file;
9296
9297		/*
9298		 * Write the backup-end xlog record
9299		 */
9300		XLogBeginInsert();
9301		XLogRegisterData(&state->startpoint,
9302						 sizeof(state->startpoint));
9303		state->stoppoint = XLogInsert(RM_XLOG_ID, XLOG_BACKUP_END);
9304
9305		/*
9306		 * Given that we're not in recovery, InsertTimeLineID is set and can't
9307		 * change, so we can read it without a lock.
9308		 */
9309		state->stoptli = XLogCtl->InsertTimeLineID;
9310
9311		/*
9312		 * Force a switch to a new xlog segment file, so that the backup is
9313		 * valid as soon as archiver moves out the current segment file.
9314		 */
9315		RequestXLogSwitch(false);
9316
9317		state->stoptime = (pg_time_t) time(NULL);
9318
9319		/*
9320		 * Write the backup history file
9321		 */
9322		XLByteToSeg(state->startpoint, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
9323		BackupHistoryFilePath(histfilepath, state->stoptli, _logSegNo,
9324							  state->startpoint, wal_segment_size);
9325		fp = AllocateFile(histfilepath, "w");
9326		if (!fp)
9327			ereport(ERROR,
9328					(errcode_for_file_access(),
9329					 errmsg("could not create file \"%s\": %m",
9330							histfilepath)));
9331
9332		/* Build and save the contents of the backup history file */
9333		history_file = build_backup_content(state, true);
9334		fprintf(fp, "%s", history_file);
9335		pfree(history_file);
9336
9337		if (fflush(fp) || ferror(fp) || FreeFile(fp))
9338			ereport(ERROR,
9339					(errcode_for_file_access(),
9340					 errmsg("could not write file \"%s\": %m",
9341							histfilepath)));
9342
9343		/*
9344		 * Clean out any no-longer-needed history files.  As a side effect,
9345		 * this will post a .ready file for the newly created history file,
9346		 * notifying the archiver that history file may be archived
9347		 * immediately.
9348		 */
9349		CleanupBackupHistory();
9350	}
9351
9352	/*
9353	 * If archiving is enabled, wait for all the required WAL files to be
9354	 * archived before returning. If archiving isn't enabled, the required WAL
9355	 * needs to be transported via streaming replication (hopefully with
9356	 * wal_keep_size set high enough), or some more exotic mechanism like
9357	 * polling and copying files from pg_wal with script. We have no knowledge
9358	 * of those mechanisms, so it's up to the user to ensure that he gets all
9359	 * the required WAL.
9360	 *
9361	 * We wait until both the last WAL file filled during backup and the
9362	 * history file have been archived, and assume that the alphabetic sorting
9363	 * property of the WAL files ensures any earlier WAL files are safely
9364	 * archived as well.
9365	 *
9366	 * We wait forever, since archive_command is supposed to work and we
9367	 * assume the admin wanted his backup to work completely. If you don't
9368	 * wish to wait, then either waitforarchive should be passed in as false,
9369	 * or you can set statement_timeout.  Also, some notices are issued to
9370	 * clue in anyone who might be doing this interactively.
9371	 */
9372
9373	if (waitforarchive &&
9374		((!backup_stopped_in_recovery && XLogArchivingActive()) ||
9375		 (backup_stopped_in_recovery && XLogArchivingAlways())))
9376	{
9377		XLByteToPrevSeg(state->stoppoint, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
9378		XLogFileName(lastxlogfilename, state->stoptli, _logSegNo,
9379					 wal_segment_size);
9380
9381		XLByteToSeg(state->startpoint, _logSegNo, wal_segment_size);
9382		BackupHistoryFileName(histfilename, state->stoptli, _logSegNo,
9383							  state->startpoint, wal_segment_size);
9384
9385		seconds_before_warning = 60;
9386		waits = 0;
9387
9388		while (XLogArchiveIsBusy(lastxlogfilename) ||
9389			   XLogArchiveIsBusy(histfilename))
9390		{
9391			CHECK_FOR_INTERRUPTS();
9392
9393			if (!reported_waiting && waits > 5)
9394			{
9395				ereport(NOTICE,
9396						(errmsg("base backup done, waiting for required WAL segments to be archived")));
9397				reported_waiting = true;
9398			}
9399
9400			(void) WaitLatch(MyLatch,
9401							 WL_LATCH_SET | WL_TIMEOUT | WL_EXIT_ON_PM_DEATH,
9402							 1000L,
9403							 WAIT_EVENT_BACKUP_WAIT_WAL_ARCHIVE);
9404			ResetLatch(MyLatch);
9405
9406			if (++waits >= seconds_before_warning)
9407			{
9408				seconds_before_warning *= 2;	/* This wraps in >10 years... */
9409				ereport(WARNING,
9410						(errmsg("still waiting for all required WAL segments to be archived (%d seconds elapsed)",
9411								waits),
9412						 errhint("Check that your \"archive_command\" is executing properly.  "
9413								 "You can safely cancel this backup, "
9414								 "but the database backup will not be usable without all the WAL segments.")));
9415			}
9416		}
9417
9418		ereport(NOTICE,
9419				(errmsg("all required WAL segments have been archived")));
9420	}
9421	else if (waitforarchive)
9422		ereport(NOTICE,
9423				(errmsg("WAL archiving is not enabled; you must ensure that all required WAL segments are copied through other means to complete the backup")));
9424}
9425
9426
9427/*
9428 * do_pg_abort_backup: abort a running backup
9429 *
9430 * This does just the most basic steps of do_pg_backup_stop(), by taking the
9431 * system out of backup mode, thus making it a lot more safe to call from
9432 * an error handler.
9433 *
9434 * 'arg' indicates that it's being called during backup setup; so
9435 * sessionBackupState has not been modified yet, but runningBackups has
9436 * already been incremented.  When it's false, then it's invoked as a
9437 * before_shmem_exit handler, and therefore we must not change state
9438 * unless sessionBackupState indicates that a backup is actually running.
9439 *
9440 * NB: This gets used as a PG_ENSURE_ERROR_CLEANUP callback and
9441 * before_shmem_exit handler, hence the odd-looking signature.
9442 */
9443void
9444do_pg_abort_backup(int code, Datum arg)
9445{
9446	bool		during_backup_start = DatumGetBool(arg);
9447
9448	/* If called during backup start, there shouldn't be one already running */
9449	Assert(!during_backup_start || sessionBackupState == SESSION_BACKUP_NONE);
9450
9451	if (during_backup_start || sessionBackupState != SESSION_BACKUP_NONE)
9452	{
9453		WALInsertLockAcquireExclusive();
9454		Assert(XLogCtl->Insert.runningBackups > 0);
9455		XLogCtl->Insert.runningBackups--;
9456
9457		sessionBackupState = SESSION_BACKUP_NONE;
9458		WALInsertLockRelease();
9459
9460		if (!during_backup_start)
9461			ereport(WARNING,
9462					errmsg("aborting backup due to backend exiting before pg_backup_stop was called"));
9463	}
9464}
9465
9466/*
9467 * Register a handler that will warn about unterminated backups at end of
9468 * session, unless this has already been done.
9469 */
9470void
9471register_persistent_abort_backup_handler(void)
9472{
9473	static bool already_done = false;
9474
9475	if (already_done)
9476		return;
9477	before_shmem_exit(do_pg_abort_backup, DatumGetBool(false));
9478	already_done = true;
9479}
9480
9481/*
9482 * Get latest WAL insert pointer
9483 */
9484XLogRecPtr
9485GetXLogInsertRecPtr(void)
9486{
9487	XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
9488	uint64		current_bytepos;
9489
9490	SpinLockAcquire(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
9491	current_bytepos = Insert->CurrBytePos;
9492	SpinLockRelease(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
9493
9494	return XLogBytePosToRecPtr(current_bytepos);
9495}
9496
9497/*
9498 * Get latest WAL record end pointer
9499 */
9500XLogRecPtr
9501GetXLogInsertEndRecPtr(void)
9502{
9503	XLogCtlInsert *Insert = &XLogCtl->Insert;
9504	uint64		current_bytepos;
9505
9506	SpinLockAcquire(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
9507	current_bytepos = Insert->CurrBytePos;
9508	SpinLockRelease(&Insert->insertpos_lck);
9509
9510	return XLogBytePosToEndRecPtr(current_bytepos);
9511}
9512
9513/*
9514 * Get latest WAL write pointer
9515 */
9516XLogRecPtr
9517GetXLogWriteRecPtr(void)
9518{
9519	RefreshXLogWriteResult(LogwrtResult);
9520
9521	return LogwrtResult.Write;
9522}
9523
9524/*
9525 * Returns the redo pointer of the last checkpoint or restartpoint. This is
9526 * the oldest point in WAL that we still need, if we have to restart recovery.
9527 */
9528void
9529GetOldestRestartPoint(XLogRecPtr *oldrecptr, TimeLineID *oldtli)
9530{
9531	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_SHARED);
9532	*oldrecptr = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.redo;
9533	*oldtli = ControlFile->checkPointCopy.ThisTimeLineID;
9534	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
9535}
9536
9537/* Thin wrapper around ShutdownWalRcv(). */
9538void
9539XLogShutdownWalRcv(void)
9540{
9541	ShutdownWalRcv();
9542	ResetInstallXLogFileSegmentActive();
9543}
9544
9545/* Enable WAL file recycling and preallocation. */
9546void
9547SetInstallXLogFileSegmentActive(void)
9548{
9549	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
9550	XLogCtl->InstallXLogFileSegmentActive = true;
9551	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
9552}
9553
9554/* Disable WAL file recycling and preallocation. */
9555void
9556ResetInstallXLogFileSegmentActive(void)
9557{
9558	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_EXCLUSIVE);
9559	XLogCtl->InstallXLogFileSegmentActive = false;
9560	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
9561}
9562
9563bool
9564IsInstallXLogFileSegmentActive(void)
9565{
9566	bool		result;
9567
9568	LWLockAcquire(ControlFileLock, LW_SHARED);
9569	result = XLogCtl->InstallXLogFileSegmentActive;
9570	LWLockRelease(ControlFileLock);
9571
9572	return result;
9573}
9574
9575/*
9576 * Update the WalWriterSleeping flag.
9577 */
9578void
9579SetWalWriterSleeping(bool sleeping)
9580{
9581	SpinLockAcquire(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
9582	XLogCtl->WalWriterSleeping = sleeping;
9583	SpinLockRelease(&XLogCtl->info_lck);
9584}
